title: PDEs 5: Fractional Sobolev spaces
section: 7 Some applications: Trace, embeddings and regularity
text:
7 Some applications: Trace, embeddings and regularity

7.1 Trace operator
Consider \(f \in L^p(\Omega )\) a PDE of the form
\[
\begin{aligned}
\Ll u =f \text{ in } \Omega , \quad \restr{u}{\partial \Omega }= g.
\end{aligned}
\tag{11}\]
Then, it is necessary to know exactly what boundary data \(g\) is admissible. Suppose that \(\Ll\) is of order \(k\) so we require \(u \in W^{k,p}(\Omega )\). Will (11) have a solution? To be able to answer this question, we need to know the image of the trace operator. If \(g \notin \operatorname{Tr}(W^{k,p}(\Omega ))\), then there is no hope of finding a solution. The following theorem characterizes the image of the trace operator and can be found in (Agranovich 2015) page 228 and (Leoni 2023, 229:350, 492).

Theorem 8 (Fractional trace theorem) Let \(\Omega \subset \R^d\) be open with \(C^{k,1}\) boundary. Then, for all \(p\in (1,\infty), s\in (1/p,k)\), the trace operator \(\operatorname{Tr}\) can be extended from \(C(\overline{\Omega } )\) to a bounded operator
\[
\begin{aligned}
\operatorname{Tr}: H^{s,p}(\Omega ) \to B^{s-1/p,p}(\partial\Omega), \quad \operatorname{Tr}: B^{s,p}(\Omega ) \to B^{s-1/p,p}(\partial\Omega).
\end{aligned}
\]
Furthermore, given \(g \in B^{s-1/p,p}(\partial\Omega)\), there exists \(u \in W^{s,p}(\Omega )\) such that \(\operatorname{Tr}(u)=g\) with
\[
\begin{aligned}
\norm{u}_{W^{s,p}(\Omega )}\lesssim \norm{g}_{B^{s-1/p,p}(\partial\Omega)}.
\end{aligned}
\]

Note that, since we have equality of \(W^{s,p}(\Omega )\) with \(H^{s,p}(\Omega )\) and \(B^{s,p}(\Omega )\) for integer and non-integer \(s\) respectively, we can also extend
\[
\begin{aligned}
\operatorname{Tr}: W^{s,p}(\Omega ) \to B^{s-1/p,p}(\partial\Omega).
\end{aligned}
\]


7.2 Fractional Sobolev embeddings
In this section, we state the fractional analogue of the Sobolev embedding theorems for regularity \(\gamma \in (0,1)\). Here, the analogous of the exponent \(p_k^{*}\) is

Definition 10 Given \(p \in [1,\infty)\) and \(s>0\), with \(s\in (d/p, \infty)\), we define the Sobolev critical exponent \(p_s^*\) by
\[
\begin{aligned}
\frac{1}{p_s ^*}:=\frac{1}{p}-\frac{s }{d}.
\end{aligned}
\]

The natural extension of the Sobolev embedding theorem to the fractional case is the following. First, we introduce the following notation for the fractional seminorm.
\[
\begin{aligned}
\abs{u}_{W^{\gamma, p}(\Omega )}:=\int_{\R^d}\int_{\R^d}\frac{\abs{u(x+y)-u(y)}}{\abs{x}^{d+\gamma p}}\d x \d y.
\end{aligned}
\]
See (Leoni 2023) page 262 for the following result.

Theorem 9 (Fractional Sobolev-Gagliardo-Niremberg) Given an extension domain \(\Omega\) for \(W^{\gamma,p}\) and \(\gamma \in (0,1), p \in [1,\infty)\), it holds that
\[
\begin{aligned}
\|u\|_{L^{p_\gamma^*}(\Omega )} \lesssim \|u\|_{W^{\gamma, p}(\Omega )}, \quad\forall \gamma <  \frac{d}{p}
\end{aligned}
\]
In particular, by interpolation, for all \(q \in [p,p_\gamma^*]\).
\[
\begin{aligned}
\|u\|_{L^{q}(\Omega )} \lesssim \norm{u}_{W^{\gamma, p}(\Omega )}, \quad\forall \gamma <  \frac{d}{p}.
\end{aligned}
\]

The critical case \(\gamma=\frac{d}{p}\) corresponding to \(p_\gamma^*=\infty\) is now (see (Leoni 2023) page 265)

Theorem 10 Given an extension domain \(\Omega\) for \(W^{\gamma,p}\) and \(\gamma \in (0,1), q \in [p,\infty)\), it holds that
\[
\begin{aligned}
\|u\|_{L^q(\Omega )} \lesssim\|u\|_{W^{\gamma, p}(\Omega)}, \quad \gamma =  \frac{d}{p}.
\end{aligned}
\]


Exercise 9 Using Theorem 9 prove Theorem 10







TipHint





We are in the subcritical case for \(r<d/p=\gamma\). Extend \(u\) to \(\R^d\) to form \(\tl{u}\). Then, by Proposition 1, we have
\[
\begin{aligned}
\|u\|_{L^{p_r^*}(\Omega )}\leq \|u\|_{L^{p_r^*}(\R^d )}  \lesssim \norm{u}_{W^{r, p}(\R^d )}\leq \norm{u}_{W^{\gamma, p}(\R^d  )}\lesssim \norm{u}_{W^{\gamma, p}(\Omega )}.
\end{aligned}
\]
Conclude by finding \(r\) such that \(p_r^*=q\).



The supercritical case \(\gamma>d/p\) can be found in (Agranovich 2015) page 224 and (Leoni 2023) page 275. To simplify the notation we use the convention that \(C^r\) stands for \(C^{\lfloor r \rfloor, r-\lfloor r \rfloor}\).

Theorem 11 (Morrey’s fractional embedding) Let \(\Omega\) be an extension domain for \(W^{s,p}\), we have a continuous embedding
\[
\begin{aligned}
W^{s,p}(\Omega) \hookrightarrow  C^{s-d/p}(\Omega), \quad\forall s> \frac{d}{p}.
\end{aligned}
\]
This embedding also holds for \(s=d / p\) provided that \(s-d/p\) is non-integer.

As in the non-fractional case, one can also consider higher smoothness on the right-hand side (see (Leoni 2023) page 290).

Theorem 12 (Sobolev embedding into higher smoothness) Let \(\Omega\) be an extension domain for \(W^{\gamma,p}\). Then, given \(p_1, p_2 \in [1,\infty)\) and \(0\leq \gamma_1<\gamma_2< 1\), it holds that
\[
\begin{aligned}
\norm{u}_{W^{\gamma_1,p_1}(\Omega )}\lesssim \norm{u}_{W^{\gamma_2,p_2}(\Omega )}, \quad\forall \gamma_2 - \frac{d}{p_2} = \gamma_1 - \frac{d}{p_1}.
\end{aligned}
\]


Exercise 10 Justify via a scaling argument that the condition \(\gamma_2 - \frac{d}{p_2} = \gamma_1 - \frac{d}{p_1}\) is necessary for the embedding in Theorem 12.







TipHint





Extend to a function on \(\R^d\), then swap \(u\) with \(u_\lambda (x):=u(\lambda x)\)and apply the change of variables \((x,y)\to \lambda (x,y)\).



Finally, interpolation results are also possible. See (Leoni 2023) page 300.

Theorem 13 Let \(\Omega\) be an extension domain for \(W^{\gamma,p}\) and consider \(p_1,p_2 \in (1,\infty), 0 \leq \gamma_1<\gamma_2 \leq 1,\) and \(0<\theta<1\). Then
\[
\begin{aligned}
\|u\|_{W^{s, p}(\R^d)} \lesssim\|u\|_{W^{\gamma_1, p_1}(\R^d)}^\theta\|u\|_{W^{\gamma_2, p_2}(\R^d)}^{1-\theta}
\end{aligned}
\]
for all \(u \in W^{\gamma_1, p_1}(\R^d) \cap W^{\gamma_2, p_2}(\R^d)\), where \(\frac{1}{p}=\frac{\theta}{p_1}+\frac{1-\theta}{p_2}\) and \(s=\theta \gamma_1+\) \((1-\theta) \gamma_2.\)

The above results can also be formulated in terms of the Sobolev seminorm. For example, Theorem 13 can be formulated as
\[
\begin{aligned}
\abs{u}_{W^{s, p}(\R^d)} \lesssim \abs{u}_{W^{\gamma_1, p_1}(\R^d)}^\theta\abs{u}_{W^{\gamma_2, p_2}(\R^d)}^{1-\theta}.
\end{aligned}
\]
Higher order embeddings and interpolations can be obtained from the previous cases with regularity parameter below 1 in combination with the integer case.

Exercise 11 (Fractional Sobolev Embeddings) Let \(\Omega \subset \mathbb{R}^d\) be an extension domain for \(W^{s,p}\). Given \(s \ge 0\) and \(p \in [1, \infty)\), set \(k=\lfloor s \rfloor\) and \(\gamma =s-k\). The following continuous embeddings hold:
\[
\begin{aligned}
& W^{s, p}(\Omega) \hookrightarrow L^q(\Omega), \quad \forall q \in [p, p_s^*] \quad \text{ if } s<\frac{d}{p} \\
& W^{s, p}(\Omega) \hookrightarrow L^q(\Omega), \quad \forall q \in [p, \infty) \quad \text{ if } s=\frac{d}{p} \\
& W^{s, p}(\Omega) \hookrightarrow C^{m, \alpha}(\bar{\Omega})  \text{ if } s>\frac{d}{p} \text{ and } s-\frac{d}{p} \notin \mathbb{N},
\end{aligned}
\]
where \(\frac{1}{p_s^*} = \frac{1}{p} - \frac{s}{d}\), \(m = \lfloor s - d/p \rfloor\), and \(\alpha = s - d/p - m\) if \(s - d/p \notin \mathbb{N}\) or \(\alpha \in (0, 1)\) is any otherwise.







TipHint





Observe the algebraic relation:
\[
\begin{aligned}
\frac{1}{(p_\gamma ^*)_k^*}=\frac{1}{p_\gamma^*}-\frac{k}{d}=\frac{1}{p}-\frac{s}{d}= \frac{1}{p_s^*}.
\end{aligned}
\]
The result follows from applying Theorem 9 and Theorem 11 sequentially with the integer-case embeddings. For the first (the subcritical) case, the fractional embedding Theorem 9 yields:
\[
\begin{aligned}
\norm{D^\alpha u}_{L^{p_\gamma ^*}(\Omega )}\lesssim \norm{D^\alpha u}_{W^{\gamma ,p}(\Omega )} \lesssim \norm{u}_{W^{s,p}(\Omega )}, \quad \forall \abs{\alpha}\leq k.
\end{aligned}
\]
Applying the integer Sobolev embedding to \(W^{k, p_\gamma^*}(\Omega)\), we conclude:
\[
\begin{aligned}
\norm{u}_{L^{p_s^*}(\Omega)}=\norm{u}_{L^{(p_\gamma ^*)_k ^*}(\Omega)}\lesssim \norm{u}_{W^{k,p_\gamma ^*}(\Omega)}\lesssim \norm{u}_{W^{s,p}(\Omega)}.
\end{aligned}
\]
For the second (critical) case \(s=d/p\), the same bootstrapping yields \(W^{s,p}(\Omega) \hookrightarrow W^{k, p_\gamma^*}(\Omega)\). Since \(k = d/p_\gamma^*\), the integer critical embedding applies.
For the supercritical case (\(s>d/p\)), apply the fractional Morrey embedding directly, or bootstrap to an integer space \(W^{k, \tilde{p}}(\Omega)\) where \(k > d/\tilde{p}\), and apply the integer Morrey embedding to extract the precise Hölder regularity \(C^{m, \alpha}(\bar{\Omega})\).



Below we plot the Sobolev critical exponent \(p_s^*\) for \(p=2\) and \(d=1,2,3\). As we can see, it decreases with \(d\) and increases with \(s\). This means that the larger the dimension the more regularity we need to obtain a bound on the same \(L^q(\R^d)\) norm. The integrability increasing to infinity around the critical threshold \(s=d/p\).






Figure 1: Sobolev critical exponent for \(p=2\) and \(d=1,2,3\)




Exercise 12 Let \(\Omega\) be an extension domain for \(W^{s_1,p}\). Show that, given \(p_1, p_2 \in [1,\infty)\) and \(0 \leq s_2<s_1 <\infty\), it holds that
\[
\begin{aligned}
W^{s_1, p_1}(\Omega ) \hookrightarrow W^{s_2, p_2}(\Omega ), \quad s_1 - \frac{d}{p_1} = s_2 - \frac{d}{p_2}.
\end{aligned}
\]







TipHint





Set \(k_i =\left\lfloor s_i \right\rfloor\) and \(\gamma_i =s_i-k_i\). Apply Theorem 12 to the derivatives of order up to \(k_2\) to obtain
\[
\begin{aligned}
\norm{D^\alpha u}_{W^{\gamma_2, p_2}(\Omega )}\lesssim \norm{D^\alpha u}_{W^{\gamma _1,p_1}(\Omega )} \leq \norm{u}_{W^{s_1,p_1}(\Omega )}, \quad \forall \abs{\alpha}\leq k_2.
\end{aligned}
\]
Deduce that
\[
\begin{aligned}
\norm{u}_{W^{s_2,p_2}(\Omega )}\lesssim \norm{u}_{W^{s_1,p_1}(\Omega )}.
\end{aligned}
\]



For more higher order embeddings see also (Leoni 2023) Section 11.4. Finally, embeddings can be similarly formulated for Besov spaces. See (Sawano et al. 2018) page 219 for the following result.

Theorem 14 (Embedding for Besov spaces) Let \(\Omega\) be an extension domain, and consider \(1 \leq p_1<p_2 \leq \infty, - \infty <s_2<s_1<\infty\). Then,
\[
\begin{aligned}
B^{s_1,p_1}(\Omega) \hookrightarrow B^{s_2,p_2}(\Omega), \quad s_1-\frac{n}{p_1}=s_2-\frac{n}{p_2} .
\end{aligned}
\]

This and other results can be formulated for the more general spaces \(B^{s,p}_q\). Where \(B^{s,p}= B^{s,p}_p\). See, (Triebel 1992), (Agranovich 2015), (Sawano et al. 2018).
We conclude this post by commenting that given a second-order PDE with smooth coefficients, such as
\[
\begin{aligned}
- \Delta u =f \text{ in } \Omega , \quad \restr{u}{\partial \Omega }= 0.
\end{aligned}
\]
One expects that \(u\) is two degrees more regular than \(f\). That is, if \(f \in W^{s,p}(\Omega )\), then we should have \(u \in W^{s+2,p}(\Omega )\). This is indeed the case locally. However, to obtain smoothness up to the boundary, one also needs \(\partial \Omega\) to be regular enough. In this case, Lipschitz continuity of \(\Omega\) is not sufficient, even if \(f \in C^\infty(\overline{\Omega } )\) (see for example (Savaré 1998)). We may comment on this later in a future post.

8 References


Agranovich, Mikhail S. 2015. Sobolev Spaces, Their Generalizations and Elliptic Problems in Smooth and Lipschitz Domains. Springer. https://link.springer.com/book/10.1007/978-3-319-14648-5.


Biccari, Umberto, Mahamadi Warma, and Enrique Zuazua. 2018. “Local Regularity for Fractional Heat Equations.” Recent Advances in PDEs: Analysis, Numerics and Control: In Honor of Prof. Fernández-Cara’s 60th Birthday, 233–49. https://link.springer.com/chapter/10.1007/978-3-319-97613-6_12.


Brezis, Haim, and Haim Brézis. 2011. Functional Analysis, Sobolev Spaces and Partial Differential Equations. Vol. 2. 3. Springer.


Di Nezza, Eleonora, Giampiero Palatucci, and Enrico Valdinoci. 2012. “Hitchhiker’s Guide to the Fractional Sobolev Spaces.” Bulletin Des Sciences Mathematiques 136 (5): 521–73. https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0007449711001254.


Evans, Lawrence C. 2022. Partial Differential Equations. Vol. 19. American Mathematical Society. https://math24.files.wordpress.com/2013/02/partial-differential-equations-by-evans.pdf.


Leoni, Giovanni. 2017. A First Course in Sobolev Spaces. American Mathematical Soc. https://www.google.co.uk/books/edition/A_First_Course_in_Sobolev_Spaces/qoA8DwAAQBAJ?hl=en&gbpv=0.


———. 2023. A First Course in Fractional Sobolev Spaces. Vol. 229. American Mathematical Society. https://www.google.co.uk/books/edition/A_First_Course_in_Fractional_Sobolev_Spa/lh2_EAAAQBAJ?hl=en&gbpv=1&dq=giovanni+leoni+fractional+sobolev&pg=PP1&printsec=frontcover.


Savaré, Giuseppe. 1998. “Regularity Results for Elliptic Equations in Lipschitz Domains.” Journal of Functional Analysis 152 (1): 176–201. https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S002212369793158X/pdf?md5=c646200fe7117dd7d25d27439f36b342&pid=1-s2.0-S002212369793158X-main.pdf.


Sawano, Yoshihiro et al. 2018. Theory of Besov Spaces. Vol. 56. Springer. https://link.springer.com/book/10.1007/978-981-13-0836-9.


Stein, Elias M. 1970. Singular Integrals and Differentiability Properties of Functions. Princeton university press. https://www.degruyter.com/document/doi/10.1515/9781400883882/html.


Taylor, Michael. 2013. Partial Differential Equations II: Qualitative Studies of Linear Equations. Vol. 116. Springer Science & Business Media.


Triebel, Hans. 1992. Theory of Function Spaces II. Vol. 84. Birkhäuser. https://link.springer.com/book/10.1007/978-3-0346-0419-2.

title: PDEs 3: Sobolev spaces
text:
1 Three line summary

Given open \(U \subset \R^d\) the Sobolev spaces \(W^{k,p}(U)\) are complete spaces of ** weakly differentiable** functions.
Smooth functions are dense in \(W^{k,p}(\Omega)\) if \(\Omega\) is bounded and has \(C^1\) boundary. This lets us manipulate Sobolev functions as if they were smooth and then take limits. In particular, we can extend and restrict functions to \(\partial \Omega\).
Functions in \(W^{k,p}(\Omega)\) enjoy various inequalities. Cashing in differentiability for integrability we can compactly embed
\[
  \begin{aligned}
  W^{k,p}(\Omega) \hookrightarrow L^q(\Omega).
  \end{aligned}
  \]
Where \(q\equiv q(d,k,p)\) decreases with the dimension \(d\), increases with differentiability \(k\) and integrability \(p\), and is larger than \(p\).

title: PDEs 3: Sobolev spaces
text:
2 Why should I care?
Sobolev spaces allow us to extend the notion of differentiability to a wider class of functions. The fact that these spaces are complete and compactly embedded in \(L^q\) spaces is an important tool to extract convergent sub-sequences. This is useful when solving differential equations as a common technique is to take a Cauchy sequence whose limit is the solution to the equation.

title: PDEs 3: Sobolev spaces
text:
3 Notation

In this post we will be dealing with functions over a variety of domains. To facilitate interpretation of the notation we will stick to the convention that \(K\) is a compact set, \(U, V\) are open sets, and \(\Omega\) is an open bounded set with \(C^1\) boundary.
Given a topological space \(X\) and a subset \(A \subset X\) we abbreviate \(A\) is dense in \(X\) with the topology of \(X\) by
\[
  \begin{aligned}
  \overline{A}=X.
  \end{aligned}
  \]
We stress that in practice \(X\) may be itself a subset of some larger space \(Y\) (for example \(X= H^s(\R^d)\) and \(Y= L^p(\R^d)\)) . However, the above notation will always mean the closure with the topology of \(X\) and not \(Y\).
A related notation is we will write given \(a_n \in  A\)
\[
  \begin{aligned}
  \lim_{n \to \infty}a_n =x \in X.
  \end{aligned}
  \]
To mean the limit in the topology of \(X\).
Given two sets \(A,B\) we write \(A \Subset B\) and say that \(A\) is compactly included in \(B\) if \(\overline{A}\) is compact and strictly included in \(B\).
We also write
\[
  \begin{aligned}
  A+B=\set{x+y:x \in A, y \in  B} .
  \end{aligned}
  \]
Given a topological vector space \(X\) we write \(X'\) for the dual of \(X\) and denote given \(w \in X', \varphi \in X\) the duality pairing as
\[
  \begin{aligned}
  (\varphi,w):= w(\varphi) .
  \end{aligned}
  \]
We will always write \(\alpha\) for a multi-index \(\alpha \in \N^d\) and use the notation
\[
  \begin{aligned}
  D^\alpha f := \partial _1^{\alpha_1}\cdots \partial _d^{\alpha_d}.
  \end{aligned}
  \]
In the case \(\alpha =0\) we use the convention \(D^0 =f\).
Given a space of functions \(X\) with domain \(A\) and \(B \subset A\) we write
\[
  \begin{aligned}
  \restr{X}{B}:=\set{\restr{f}{B}: f\in X} .
  \end{aligned}
  \]
Given two quantities \(M,N\) we write \(M \lesssim N\) to mean that there exists some constant \(C\) independent of \(M\) and \(N\) such that \(M \leq C N\).
We write \(B_r(x)\) for the ball centered at \(x\) with radius \(r\) and \(B_r\) if \(x=0\). The space where the ball is contained depending on context.

title: PDEs 3: Sobolev spaces
text:
4 Introduction
Hi everyone, welcome back to another post on our series on PDEs. This post goes into some depth on the main space of functions we will work with. Since the post is rather long (yes it has appendices, no that wasn’t planned) I decided to include a pdf at the end of the post in case it is easier to navigate. That said, in the future we will want to solve a differential equation of the form
\[
\begin{aligned}
\Ll u = f\quad  \mathrm{ on  }\quad  D .
\end{aligned}
\tag{1}\]
Where \(D\) is some domain in \(\R^d\). In a previous post on the Fourier transform we saw how to define the Sobolev spaces \(H^s(D)\) when \(D\) is the whole Euclidean space \(\R^d\) or the torus \(\T^d\). These spaces correspond to \(s\)-times weakly differentiable functions and we saw how these spaces could help us solve (1). However, in practice \(D\) may be an open set in \(\R^d\) or even some \(d\)-dimensional manifold with a boundary condition
\[
\begin{aligned}
\restr{u}{\partial  D}= g.
\end{aligned}
\tag{2}\]
Note that equation (2) is a priori ill-defined as the Lebesgue measure of \(\partial D \subset \R^d\) is zero. Thus, it is necessary to extend the theory to a wider class of domains and to explain what we mean by the restriction of a function to its boundary of definition.

4.1 A first attempt
Suppose for example \(D=U\) is an open set and \(u: U \to \R\). Then, we can try to define \(H^s(U)\) using our knowledge of \(H^s(\R^d)\) by:

Extending \(u\) by zero outside of \(U\) to form
\[
\begin{aligned}
\tilde{u}(x):=\begin{cases}
u(x) & \quad x \in U     \\
0    & \quad x \not\in U
\end{cases}.
\end{aligned}
\]
Studying if \(\tilde{u} \in  H^s(\R^d)\). That is, as we saw in the previous post, checking if
\[
\begin{aligned}
\norm{\tilde{u}}_{H^s(\R^d)}^2= \int_{\R^d}\br{\xi }^{2s}\wh{\tilde{u}}(\xi )^2 \d \xi < \infty
\end{aligned}
\]
Saying that \(u \in H^s(U)\) if and only if \(\tilde{u} \in H^s(\R^d)\).

However, this runs into problems as is shown in the following example:

Example 1 Let \(U=(0,1)\) and take \(u: (0,1) \to \R\) defined to be identically equal to \(1\). Note that \(u \in C^\infty(U)\) so we expect that \(u \in H^s((0,1))\) for all \(s \in \R\). However, it holds that \(\tilde{u}= {1}_{(0,1)}\) with
\[
\begin{aligned}
\wh{\tilde{u}}(\xi )=\frac{1-e^{-2 \pi i \xi }}{2 \pi i \xi }.
\end{aligned}
\]

As we can see, by substituting in our naive definition of \(H^s(U)\) gives that \(u \in H^s((0,1))\) if and only if \(s< \frac{1}{2}\). Thus, our program of extending \(u\) by zero and studying the regularity of the extension is not going to work. The reason for this is that, by extending by zero we introduce a discontinuity on \(\tilde{u}\) at the boundary of \(U\).


4.2 A second approach
Alternatively, we could also define
\[
\begin{aligned}
H^s(U)= \restr{H^s(\R^d)}{U}:= \set{\restr{f}{u}: f \in H^s(\R^d)} .
\end{aligned}
\]
As we will see later (Exercise 20) this is a better approach. However, it is not optimal as it requires some conditions on \(U\). For example, if \(U\) is not smooth we cannot assert that \(\restr{C^\infty(\R^d)}{U}= C^\infty(U)\).

title: PDEs 3: Sobolev spaces
text:
5 Test functions and distributions

5.1 Seminorms and their topologies
We need a new approach. Motivated as in the previous post by duality we should begin by defining what is meant by a weak derivative of a function \(u\) in \(L^p(U)\). If \(u,\varphi\) are smooth functions then we have by integration by parts
\[
\begin{aligned}
(\varphi, D^\alpha u):= \int_{D} \varphi D^\alpha u  = (-1)^{\abs{\alpha} }\int_{D}(D^{\alpha} \varphi) u + \text{ boundary effects}  .
\end{aligned}
\]
In the previous post, we used that

If \(D =\R^d\) we can take as our test functions \(\varphi \in  \Ss(\R^d)\) and use that \(\varphi\) multiplied by any function in \(L^2\) (\(u\) and its derivatives) vanish at infinity to get rid of the boundary effects.
If \(D =\T^d\) we can take as our test functions \(\varphi \in  C^\infty(\T^d)\) as the boundary effect of periodic functions cancels out.

To obtain this cancellation on a general open \(D\) we need to impose that our test function \(\varphi\) vanishes in a neighborhood of the boundary. That is we need our test functions to have compact support.

Definition 1 Let \(U\) be an open set, then we define \(C_c^\infty(U)\) to be the space of smooth functions whose support is some compact set \(K \subset U\).

Another notation for \(C^\infty_c(U)\) is \(\Dd(U)\) and it is often called the space of test functions for reasons we will later see. Given a compact subset \(K \subset U\) we define for each \(k \in \N\)

Definition 2 Given a compact subset \(K\) of an open set \(U\) we define
\[
\begin{aligned}
C_c^\infty(K):= \set{\varphi \in C_c^\infty(U): \supp{\varphi} \subset K } .
\end{aligned}
\]
We endow \(C_c^\infty(K)\) with the topology generated by the countable family of seminorms
\[
\begin{aligned}
\norm{\varphi}_{C^k(K)}:= \sup_{x \in K} \sum_{\abs{\alpha}\leq k } \abs{D^\alpha \varphi}, \quad  k \in \N_{0}
\end{aligned}
\tag{3}\]

Later we will need to generate topologies when the family of seminorms is uncountable.

Definition 3 Let \(X\) be a vector space and let \(\mathcal{P}\) be a family of seminorms on \(X\). Then we define \(\tau_\Pp\), the topology generated by \(\mathcal{P}\),
\[
\begin{aligned}
B^\rho(x,\eps):=x+\rho^{-1}(B_\eps), \quad \rho \in \Pp, \eps >0
\end{aligned}
\]

That is, \(\set{B^\rho(x,\eps): \rho \in \Pp, \eps>0}\) is a sub-basis of \(\tau_\Pp\). Equivalently, \(U\) is open if and only if it is an arbitrary union of finite intersections.
The above topology is equivalent to the initial topology generated by the family
\[
\begin{aligned}
\set{\rho(\cdot-x): x\in X, \rho \in \Pp}.
\end{aligned}
\]
We include some exercises to help the reader get more used to the topology that arises. I recommended trying to solve them for a few minutes before checking the hints.

Exercise 1 Write \(\tau_{\Pp}\) for the topology generated by \(\Pp\), Show that \(\tau_{\Pp}\) is the coarsest topology that makes \(X\) into a topological vector space (TVS).







TipHint





The fact that \((X,\tau_\mathcal{P})\) is a TVS follows from the triangle inequality and homogeneity of seminorms. The fact that it is the coarsest that makes \(\rho\) continuous is that \(\rho^{-1}(B_\eps)\) must be an open neighbourhood of the origin and in a TVS, by continuity of the sum, translation of an open set must be open.




Exercise 2 Show that \((X,\tau_\mathcal{P})\) is locally convex. That is, every point has a local basis of convex sets







TipHint





Show that \(\rho^{-1}(B_\eps)\) is convex.




Exercise 3 Show that the topology \((X,\tau_\mathcal{P})\) is determined by the following property.

Given a <https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Net_(mathematics)#:~:text=
\[
  \begin{aligned}
  \lim x_\bullet =x \in  X  \iff      \rho(x_\bullet-x)\to 0 \quad\forall \rho \in \Pp.
  \end{aligned}
  \]








TipHint





The topology of any topological space is completely determined by the convergence of nets. So it is enough to show that the property holds. The implication holds by the continuity of \(\rho\), the reverse follows from being able to fit \(x_\bullet\) into any basic set \(x+\rho(B_\eps)\).




Exercise 4 Show that \(C_c^\infty(K)\) is complete and thus a <https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fr







TipHint





The topology is metrizable as the family of seminorms is countable Use Exercise 3 to show that if \(\varphi_n \in C_c^\infty(K)\) is Cauchy then the sequence of derivatives \(D^\alpha \varphi_n\) converge uniformly to \(\varphi^{(\alpha)} \in C_c^0(K)\) for all \(\alpha\). It remains to show that
\[
\begin{aligned}
\varphi^{(0)}= \lim_{n \to \infty} \varphi_n \in  C_c^\infty(K).
\end{aligned}
\]
To do so use the fundamental theorem of calculus and induction.



Using \(C_c^\infty(K)\) as a stepping stone we can build a topology on \(C_c^\infty(U)\). We use the approach in <https://terrytao.wordpress.com/2009/04/19/245c-notes-3-distributions/#:~:text=is \end{definition}

Exercise 5 Give an infinite restrictable family of seminorms.







TipHint





Valid answers include all the \(L^p(U)\) and \(C^k(U)\) norms.




Exercise 6 Show that \(C_c^\infty(\Omega)\) with the smooth topology is a locally convex topological vector space (LCTVS).







TipHint





See Exercises Exercise 12




Exercise 7 (Smooth convergence is equal to local convergence) Show that \(\varphi_n \to \varphi \in C_c^\infty(U)\) if and only if there exists a compact set \(K\) such that for all \(n\) the support of \(\varphi_n\) and \(\varphi\) are in \(K\) and
\[
\begin{aligned}
\lim_{n \to \infty}\varphi_n  =\varphi \in  C_c^\infty(K).
\end{aligned}
\]







TipHint





Given any sequence \(\mathbf{a}=\set{a_j}_{j\in \mathbb{N}} \in \R_+\) and an increasing set of compact sets \(K_j\) with \(U=\bigcup_{j\in \mathbb{N}} K_j\) show that
\[
\begin{aligned}
p_{\mathbf{a}}(\varphi):=\sup _{j \in \N}{a_j} \sum_{|\alpha| \leq j}\norm{D^\alpha \varphi(x)}_{L^\infty(U\setminus K_j)}.
\end{aligned}
\]
Is a restrictable seminorm. Why does this prevent the support of \(\varphi_n\) escaping to infinity? Now knowing all functions are supported in some \(K\) use that \(\norm{\cdot }_{C^k(K)}\) is restrictable to conclude the proof.




Exercise 8 (Completeness) Show that \(C_c^\infty(\Omega)\) with the smooth topology is complete.







TipHint





Given a Cauchy net \(\varphi_{\bullet} \in C_c^\infty(U)\) show as in Exercise 7 that the support of \(\varphi_{\bullet}\) cannot escape a compact set \(K\). Conclude using the completeness on \(C_c^\infty(K)\) and Exercise 3.




Exercise 9 Show that \(C_c^\infty(\Omega)\) with the smooth topology is Hausdorff.







TipHint





The initial topology of a set of functions that separates points is Hausdorff.




Remark 1. The topology on \(C_c^\infty(U)\) is not metrizable (note the family of seminorms used to generate it is not countable) and as a result \(C_c^\infty(U)\) is not a Fréchet space. This and more can be found in (Leoni 2017) page 286.

The construction of the topology on \(C_c^\infty(U)\) is a bit technical a more intuitive construction would be to define the family of seminorms
\[
\begin{aligned}
\norm{\varphi}_j := \sup_{\abs{\alpha} \leq j}  \norm{D^\alpha \varphi}_{L^\infty(U)} ,\quad \forall j \in \N.
\end{aligned}
\]
And then define the topology on \(C_c^\infty(U)\) to be the one generated by this family of seminorms. This has the following problem.

Exercise 10 Show that \(C_c^\infty(U)\) with the topology generated by \(\norm{\cdot }_j\) is not complete.







TipHint





Construct a sequence that is Cauchy with respect to every \(\norm{\cdot }_j\) whose support escapes to infinity.



Using the smooth topology we can now work with the dual of \(C_c^\infty(U)\)

Definition 4 We define the space of distributions to be
\[
\begin{aligned}
\Dd'(U):= (C_c^\infty(U))'.
\end{aligned}
\]
And give it the weak-\(\star\) topology.


Remark 2. The inclusions
\[
\begin{aligned}
C_c^\infty(U) \hookrightarrow C_c^\infty(\R^d) \hookrightarrow \Ss(\R^d)
\end{aligned}
\]
are continuous. As a result, \(\Ss'(\R^d) \hookrightarrow \Dd'(U)\). That is, distributions are a larger or more general class than tempered distributions.

Of course, not all smooth functions have compact support. Other (in this case Fréchet) spaces of smooth functions are
\[
\begin{aligned}
C^\infty(U)                & :=\set{\varphi: \norm{\varphi}_j:= \sup_{\abs{\alpha} \leq j}  \norm{D^\alpha \varphi}_{L^\infty(U)}<\infty,\quad \forall k \in \mathbb{N} }                  \\
C^\infty_{\mathrm{loc}}(U) & :=\set{\varphi: \norm{\varphi}_{k,K}:= \sup_{\abs{\alpha} \leq k}  \norm{D^\alpha \varphi}_{L^\infty(K)}<\infty,\quad \forall k \in \mathbb{N}, K \subset U }
\end{aligned}
\]
Where, we give them respectively the topologies generated by \(\norm{\cdot}_k\) and \(\norm{\cdot}_{k,K}\).
An equivalent characterization of \(\Dd'(U)\) (Taylor 2013, 116:241) is that, \(\omega \in  \Dd'(U)\) if and only if for all \(\varphi \in C_c^\infty(U)\) the operator \(\varphi w\) defined by \[
\begin{aligned}
(f, \varphi w):= (\varphi f, w), \quad\forall f \in C^\infty(U),
\end{aligned}
\tag{4}\]
is continuous on \(C^\infty(U)\).


5.2 Locally integrable functions as distributions
In our post on the Fourier transform we saw that integrable functions could naturally be considered as tempered distributions. The analogous is true for distributions. In this case, since our test functions are compactly supported, the functions we pair them up with only need to be locally integrable. We recall the definition

Definition 5 Given \(p\in [1,\infty]\) we write \(L^p_{\mathrm{loc}}(U)\) for the space of locally \(p\) integrable functions,
\[
\begin{aligned}
L^p_{\mathrm{loc}}(U):= \set{f: \norm{f}_{L^p(K)}< \infty, \,\text{   for all compact }  K \subset U}.
\end{aligned}
\]
And endow it with the topology generated by the family of seminorms
\[
\begin{aligned}
\rho_{K_n,p}(f):= \norm{f}_{L^p(K_n)}.
\end{aligned}
\]
Where \(K_n\) are selected such that \(\bigcup_{n \in \N} K_n =U\).

Note that \(L^q_{\mathrm{loc}}(U) \subset L^p_{\mathrm{loc} }(U)\) for all \(q \leq p\).

Exercise 11 Show that \(L^p_{\mathrm{loc} }(U)\) is a Fréchet space.







TipHint





The topology is metrizable as the family of seminorms is countable. By Exercise 3 if \(f_n \in L^p_{\mathrm{loc}}(U)\) is Cauchy then \(f_n\) is Cauchy for all \(L^p(K)\) \(D^\alpha \varphi_n\) so converges to some \(f_K \in L^p(K)\). Show that \(f_K= \restr{f}{K}\) where \(f \in L^p_{\mathrm{loc}}(U)\) to conclude the proof.




Theorem 1 (Locally integrable functions as distributions) The mapping
\[
\begin{aligned}
T:L^1_{\mathrm{loc}}(U) & \hookrightarrow  \Dd'(U) \\
u                       & \longmapsto     T_u
.
\end{aligned}
\]
Defined by
\[
\begin{aligned}
(\varphi,T_u):= \int_{U}\varphi u   , \quad\forall \varphi\in C_c^\infty(U).
\end{aligned}
\tag{5}\]
Is injective and continuous.


Proof. We begin by showing that \(T_u \in \Dd '(U)\). Firstly, the integral in (5) is finite as \(\varphi\) has compact support. Secondly, if we write \(K\) for the support of \(\varphi\), it holds that
\[
\begin{aligned}
(f,\varphi u)= \int_{K}\varphi f u \leq \norm{u}_{L^1(K)} \norm{\varphi f}_{C^0_c(K)} .
\end{aligned}
\]
Which, by our equivalent characterization of \(\Dd'(U)\) in (4) shows that \(T_u \in  \Dd'(U)\). We now show that the identification is injective. That is, if
\[
\begin{aligned}
\int_{U}\varphi u=0 , \quad\forall \varphi \in C_c^\infty(U) .
\end{aligned}
\]
Then \(u=0\). Consider a compact set \(K \subset U\) and write \(g\) for the extension of \(\mathrm{sign}(u)\) by zero outside of \(K\). Clearly \(g \subset L^1(\R^d)\). Consider an approximation to the identity \(\phi_n\) (see Appendix Section 12 ) and set
\[
\begin{aligned}
\varphi_n :=  g*\phi_n.
\end{aligned}
\]
By the approximation and smoothing of Propositions Proposition 89 we obtain a bounded sequence with \(\varphi_n \subset C_c^\infty(U)\) for \(n\) large enough and such that
\[
\begin{aligned}
\lim_{n \to \infty}\varphi_n =g \in L^1(\R^d) ; \quad \norm{\varphi_n}_{L^\infty(\R^d)} \leq \norm{g}_{L^\infty(\R^d)}=1.
\end{aligned}
\]
By the first part of the above, we may take a subsequence \(\varphi_{n_k}\) converging to \(g\) almost everywhere, and by the second we may apply the dominated convergence theorem to obtain that
\[
\begin{aligned}
0= \lim_{k \to \infty}\int_{U}\varphi_{n_k} u=\int_{K}\abs{u}.
\end{aligned}
\]
As a result, \(u=0\) vanishes on \(K\). Since \(K\) was any compact subset of \(U\) and every open set can be written as a union of compact sets we conclude that \(u=0\) as desired. The continuity of \(T\) follows from the estimate
\[
\begin{aligned}
(\varphi,T_{u} -T_v)\leq \norm{\varphi}_{C_c^k(K)}\norm{u-v}_{L^1_{\mathrm{loc}}(U)}.
\end{aligned}
\]
Where \(\varphi \in C_c^\infty(U)\) has support \(K\) (remember we are considering the weak-\(\star\) topology on \(\Dd'(U)\)).

Due to the above immersion, we will naturally consider \(L^1_{\mathrm{loc}}(U)\) as a subspace of \(\Dd'(U)\). In particular, any subspaces of \(L^1_{\mathrm{loc} }(U)\) such as \(L^p(U)\) or \(C^\infty(U)\) can also be considered as distributions.

Exercise 12 Show that the \(L^1_{\mathrm{loc}}(U)\) is not closed in \(\Dd'(U)\) .







TipHint





Show that an approximation to the identity converges to a Dirac delta \(\delta _0 \in \Dd'(U)\). However \(\delta _0 \not\in L^1_{\mathrm{loc}}(U)\).





5.3 Support of a distribution
In the continuous case, the support of a function is well-defined as the smallest closed set outside of which the function is zero. However, when working with an equivalence class of functions the definition must be amended (consider for example the support of \(0=1_{\Q}\)). This is resolved by the following definitions.

Definition 6 We say that a distribution \(w \in \Dd'(U)\) vanishes on \(V \subset U\) if
\[
\begin{aligned}
(\varphi, w)=0 , \quad\forall \varphi \in C_c^\infty(U) \text{ with } \supp{\varphi} \subset V  .
\end{aligned}
\]
And write
\[
\begin{aligned}
w =0 \text{ on } V.
\end{aligned}
\]

If a function vanishes on a collection of sets it also vanishes on their union, this extends to distributions.

Lemma 1 Let \(\set{U_\alpha}_{\alpha \subset I}\) be a collection of open sets in \(U\) and suppose that
\[
\begin{aligned}
w=0 \text{ on  } U_\alpha , \quad\forall \alpha \in I.
\end{aligned}
\]
Then \(w\) vanishes on \(U:=\bigcup_{\alpha \in  I}U_\alpha\).


Proof. Let \(\varphi\) have support in \(U\). Then, by compactness, we can extract a finite covering \(\set{U_i}_{i=1}^n\) of \(\supp{\varphi}\). Let \(\set{\rho _i}_{i=1}^n\) be a partition of unity subordinate to \(U_i\) (see Appendix Section 13). Then
\[
\begin{aligned}
(\varphi,\omega)=\qt{\sum_{i=1}^n \rho _i \varphi,w }=\sum_{i=1}^n (\rho _i \varphi,w )=0 .
\end{aligned}
\]
Since \(\varphi\) was any test function supported in \(U\) this concludes the proof.

By the just proved Lemma 1 we see that there is a largest set on which \(w\) vanishes. As a result, we can make the following definition.

Definition 7 Let \(w \in C_c^\infty(U)\) and let \(V\) be the largest open set on which \(w\) vanishes. Then, we define the support of \(w\) as
\[
\begin{aligned}
\supp{w}= V^c .
\end{aligned}
\]

Since \(L^1_{\mathrm{loc}}(U)\) is naturally included in \(\Dd'(U)\) we obtain in particular the definition of support of a function \(f \in L^1_{\mathrm{loc}}(U)\).

Exercise 13 If \(f \in L^1_{\mathrm{loc}}(U)\) the support of \(f\) is the complementary of the largest open set on which \(f\) is \(0\) almost everywhere. In particular, if \(f\) is continuous, the (distributional) support of \(f\) coincides with the classical support of \(f\).







TipHint





We saw in Theorem 1 that \(f\) is \(0\) almost everywhere on some open set if and only if it integrates to \(0\) against any test function on the open set. This shows the first part and the second follows immediately.

title: PDEs 3: Sobolev spaces
text:
6 Sobolev spaces
Now that we have built the space of distributions we can define weak derivatives of test functions just as we did with tempered distributions.

Definition 8 Given \(w \in \Dd'(U)\) we define the (weak) \(\alpha\)-th derivative by
\[
\begin{aligned}
(\varphi,D^\alpha w):=(-1)^{\abs{\alpha} }(D^\alpha \varphi, w), \quad \forall \varphi \in C_c^\infty(U).
\end{aligned}
\]


Exercise 14 Show that \(D^\alpha w \in\Dd'(U)\).







TipHint





Show that \(D^\alpha\) is continuous on \(\Dd(U)\) by using that \(\norm{\cdot }_{C^k(U)}\) are restrictable seminorms. Conclude by using that \(D^\alpha\) defined on \(\Dd'(U)\) is the adjoint of \(D^\alpha\) defined on \(\Dd(U)\)



A prerequisite for the definition to make sense is that the notion corresponds to that of classical derivative.

Exercise 15 Let \(u \in C_{\mathrm{loc}}^1(U)\) have classical derivatives \(u^{(i)}\). Then \(u^{(i)}= \partial_i u\).







TipHint





By definition of weak derivatives and the chain rule, we have the distributional equality
\[
\begin{aligned}
T_{u^{(i)}}-T_{\partial _i u}=T_{u^{(i)}-\partial _i u}=0 \in \Dd'(U)   .
\end{aligned}
\]
The result follows by the injectivity of \(T\).




Definition 9 (Sobolev spaces) Given an open set \(U \subset \R^d\), \(k \in \N\) and \(p \in [1,\infty]\) we define the Sobolev space
\[
\begin{aligned}
W^{k,p}(U):=\set{ u: D^{\alpha}u \in L^p(U) \hookrightarrow \Dd'(U), \quad\forall \abs{\alpha}\leq k   } .
\end{aligned}
\]
Where \(L^p(U)\) is identified as a subspace of \(\Dd'(U)\) by Theorem 1. We give \(W^{k,p}(U)\) the norm
\[
\begin{aligned}
\norm{u}_{W^{k,p}(U)}&:=\sum_{\abs{\alpha}\leq k } \norm{D^\alpha u}_{L^p(U)}.
\end{aligned}
\]

That is, \(W^{k,p}(U)\) is the space of \(k\)-times (weakly) differentiable functions with derivatives in \(L^p(U)\). An equivalent norm that is also sometimes used is
\[
\begin{aligned}
\norm{u}_{W^{k,p}(U)}\sim \sum_{j=0}^k \norm{\nabla^j u}_{L^p(U\to \R^{d^j})} .
\end{aligned}
\]
The local Sobolev spaces \(W^{k,p}_{\mathrm{loc}}(U)\) are defined similarly, where we now only require that
\[
\begin{aligned}
D^\alpha u \in L^p_{\text{loc}}(U) , \quad\forall \abs{\alpha}\leq k .
\end{aligned}
\]
And now generate the topology by local seminorms analogously to \(L^p_{\mathrm{loc}}(U)\).

Theorem 2 (Completeness of Sobolev spaces) For all \(k \in \N\) and \(p \in [1,\infty]\) both \(W^{k,p}(U)\) and \(W^{k,p}_{\mathrm{loc}}(U)\) are Banach spaces. If \(p \in (1,\infty)\) these spaces are also reflexive.


Proof. Let \(\set{u_n}_{n=1}^\infty\) be a Cauchy sequence in \(W^{k,p}(U)\). Then, by definition of the norm on \(W^{k,p}(U)\) the sequence of derivatives \(D^\alpha u_n\) is Cauchy in \(L^p(U)\) for each \(\abs{\alpha}\leq k\) and since \(L^p(U)\) is complete, converge to some functions \(u^{\alpha}\)
\[
\begin{aligned}
\lim_{n \to \infty} D^\alpha u_n = u^{(\alpha)} \in L^p(U) , \quad\forall \abs{\alpha} \leq k  .
\end{aligned}
\]
To conclude, it suffices to show that \(u:= u^{(0)}\) verifies
\[
\begin{aligned}
D^\alpha u= u^{(\alpha)}.
\end{aligned}
\]
This holds as, for every test function \(\varphi \in  \Dd(U)\)
\[
\begin{aligned}
\int_{U} u^{(\alpha)}\varphi =\lim_{n \to \infty}\int_{U} u_n^{(\alpha)}\varphi=\lim_{n \to \infty}(-1)^{\abs{\alpha} }\int_{U} u_nD^\alpha\varphi=(-1)^{\abs{\alpha} }\int_{U} uD^\alpha\varphi  .
\end{aligned}
\]
Where the first and last inequality follows from the continuous immersion of \(L^p(U)\) in \(\Dd'(U)\) (Hölder’s inequality). Reflexivity follows from the fact that the mapping
\[
\begin{aligned}
T: W^{k,p}(U) & \longrightarrow (L^p(U))_{\abs{\alpha}\leq k }
\\
u             & \longmapsto     (D^{\alpha}(u))_{\abs{\alpha}\leq k }
.
\end{aligned}
\]
Is an isometry, so \(\Im(T)\) is closed in the reflexive Banach space \((L^p(U))_{\abs{\alpha}\leq k }\) and thus reflexive (see (Brezis and Brézis 2011) page 70). The case of \(W^{k,p}_{\mathrm{loc}}(U)\) is proved identically now working with the local seminorms.

We now show some relevant properties of the weak derivative all of which are to be expected knowing the classical case. These can be greatly generalized with tools we will later develop.

Proposition 1 (Properties) Let \(u \in W^{k,p}(U)\). Then it holds that

Leibniz rule: given \(\varphi \in C_c^1(U)\) it holds that
\[
\begin{aligned}
\partial _{i} (u \varphi)= \partial_i u \varphi+ u \partial _i \varphi .
\end{aligned}
\]
For \(U = \R^d\), the translation \(\tau_y u(x):=u(x-y) \in W^{k,p}(\R^d)\) with \(D^\alpha \tau_y u = \tau_y D^\alpha u\).
Let \(u \in W^{k,p}(\R^d)\) and \(v \in L^1(\R^d)\). Then
\[
\begin{aligned}
D^\alpha (v*u)= v* D^\alpha u
\end{aligned}
\]



Proof. Points 1 and 2 follow by the definition of weak derivative and the relevant properties for classical derivatives. The third property follows from the second as, by Fubini,
\[
\begin{aligned}
& (v*u,D^\alpha\varphi )  = \int_{\R^d}v(y)\qt{\int_{\R^d}D^\alpha \varphi(x) u(x-y) \d x } \d y \\&
=(-1)^{\abs{\alpha} } \int_{\R^d}v(y)  \qt{\int_{\R^d}\varphi(x) D^\alpha u(x-y) \d x } \d y             =(-1)^{\abs{\alpha} }(v*D^\alpha u, \varphi).
\end{aligned}
\]

A natural question is what relationship there is between Sobolev functions and classical derivatives. For example, in \(1\) dimension a classical result is that \(u \in W^{1,1}(a,b)\) if and only if \(u\) is absolutely continuous and has derivative almost everywhere. A more general result is as follows.

Proposition 2 (Absolute continuity on lines) The following are equivalent

\(u \in W^{1,p}_{\mathrm{loc}}(U)\).
There exists a representative of \(u\) in \(L^p_{\mathrm{loc}}(U)\) that is almost everywhere differentiable with classical derivatives \(u^{(i)} \in L^p_{\mathrm{loc}}(U)\) and, given \(V \Subset U\) it holds that this representative is absolutely continuous on almost all (with respect to the Lebesgue measure on \(\R^{d-1}\)) line segments in \(V\) parallel to the coordinate axis.

The above holds true if we replace \(W^{1,p}_{\mathrm{loc}}(U),L^p_{\mathrm{loc}}(U)\) with their none local counterparts \(W^{1,p}(U),L^p(U)\).

We omit the proof which can be found in (Kinnunen 2023) pages \(39-43\). The next exercise show that, perhaps somewhat unexpectedly, to have \(u \in  W^{1,p}(U)\) it is not sufficient to require that \(u\) is differentiable almost everywhere with integrable derivatives.

Example 2 The devil’s staircase \(c\) is differentiable almost everywhere with \(c'=0\).

As a result, if \(c \in W^{1,p}(0,1)\) then \(c\) would be constant (which it is not). In fact, \(c \not\in W^{1,p}(0,1)\) as it is not absolutely continuous.

title: PDEs 3: Sobolev spaces
text:
7 Smooth approximation of Sobolev functions
The definition of weak derivative requires one to integrate against smooth functions whenever trying to prove some property holds. This is somewhat cumbersome. One would much rather

Work pretending all Sobolev functions are (classically) smooth.
Manipulate them according to the standard rules of calculus.
Obtain a result that holds for all Sobolev functions (and not just the classically smooth ones).

This process can be rigorously justified by the density of various spaces of smooth functions in Sobolev spaces. Or to use Tao’s terminology, by giving ourselves an epsilon of room.
In this section, we prove the relevant results. These rely heavily on the analogous density results for functions in \(L^p(U)\) (see Appendix Section 12). As a result, they will not when \(p=\infty\). We start without making any assumptions on \(U\) and obtain two local-type results. Throughout this section we will often be switching between different open sets and, if following the proofs, making some drawings is recommended.

Theorem 3 (Local approximation by smooth functions) Let \(u \in W^{k,p}(U)\) for \(p < \infty\), denote its extension by zero \(\tilde{u}\) and let \(\set{\phi_n}_{n=1}^\infty\) be an approximation to unity. Then
\[
\begin{aligned}
u=\lim_{n \to \infty} \tilde{u}*\phi_n  \in W^{k,p}(V) , \quad\forall V \Subset U.
\end{aligned}
\]
As a result, for any \(V \Subset U\),
\[
\begin{aligned}
\overline{\restr{C_c^\infty(\R^d)}{V}}=\restr{W^{k,p}(U)}{V}\quad \text{ and } \quad \overline{\restr{C_c^\infty(\R^d)}{U}}=W_{\mathrm{loc}}^{k,p}(U)
\end{aligned}
\]


Proof. Given a compactly embedded set \(V \Subset U\) we can take \(n\) large enough so that \(V+  B \qt{0, \frac{1}{n}} \subset U\) and as a result, the convolution \(\varphi_n:= \tilde{u} * \phi_n\) verifies (see Observation Remark 6)
\[
\begin{aligned}
\varphi_n= u* \phi_n \quad  \text{ on  } V .
\end{aligned}
\]
Thus, by the third property Proposition 1, for all \(\abs{\alpha} \leq k\)
\[
\begin{aligned}
D^\alpha \varphi_n = D^\alpha u * \phi_n \quad \text{ on } V.
\end{aligned}
\]
Taking limits we conclude from Proposition 8 that
\[
\begin{aligned}
\lim_{n \to \infty}D^\alpha \varphi_n =D^\alpha u \in L^p(V) .
\end{aligned}
\]
To conclude density of \(C_c^\infty(\R^d)\) in \(\restr{W^{k,p}(U)}{V}\) consider \(K\) such that
\[
\begin{aligned}
V \subset K; \quad  K+ B_{1 /n}\subset U.
\end{aligned}
\]
And a bump function \(\eta_V \in C_c^\infty(\R^d)\) that is equal to \(1\) on \(V\) and is supported in \(K\) (this is possible by Uryshon’s lemma and a convolution). Then,
\[
\begin{aligned}
\varphi_{V,n}:= \varphi_n \eta _V \in C_c^\infty(\R^d); \quad \lim_{n \to \infty}\varphi_{V,n}=u \in W^{k,p}(V).
\end{aligned}
\]
The density of \(C_c^\infty(\R^d)\) in \(W^{k,p}_{\mathrm{loc}}(U)\) follows by taking \(V_n\) converging to \(U\) and \(u_n:= \varphi_{V_n,n}\) as then, for every compactly included \(W \Subset  U\)
\[
\begin{aligned}
\lim_{n \to \infty}u_{n}=u \in W^{k,p}(W) .
\end{aligned}
\]
This concludes the proof as by Exercise 3 local convergence is equivalent to convergence on every compactly included subset.


Remark 3. Note that, without further assumptions on \(U\) it is impossible to get a global approximation by smooth functions defined on all of \(\R^d\). This is because, the convolution \(f* \phi_n\) can only be defined on
\[
\begin{aligned}
U_{1/n}:= \set{x \in U: d(x,\partial U)> \frac{1}{n}} .
\end{aligned}
\]

The above issue disappears when \(U =\R^d\). This shows that

Theorem 4 (Global approximation in \(\R^d\)) It holds that for all \(p<\infty\),
\[
\begin{aligned}
\overline{C_c^\infty(\R^d)}=W^{k,p}(\R^d).
\end{aligned}
\]


Proof. Let \(\eta   \in C_c^\infty(\R^d)\) be equal to \(1\) on \(B_1\) and set \(\eta  _n(x):=\eta  (x /n)\). Then, given \(u \in W^{k,p}(\R^n)\) and a smooth approximation to unity \(\phi_n\) we obtain that, by the triangle inequality
\[
\begin{aligned}
u= \lim_{n \to \infty}(u*\phi_n)\eta  _n \in W^{k,p}(\R^d).
\end{aligned}
\]

The following Theorem shows a global-type approximation.

Theorem 5 (Meyers-Serrin: local till boundary approximation) Let \(U \subset \R^d\) be open, then for all \(p<\infty\)
\[
\begin{aligned}
\overline{C^\infty_{\mathrm{loc}}(U)\cap W^{k,p}(U)}=W^{k,p}(U).
\end{aligned}
\]


Proof. The proof is an instructive way of using a partition of unity to piece together a local result (in this case Theorem 3) to get a global one. Let \(\eps >0\) and consider an open covering \(\set{V_i}_{i=0}^\infty\) of \(U\) with \(V_0 =\emptyset\) and \(V_{i} \Subset V_{i+1}\). By Theorem 17 we may obtain a partition of unity \(\rho _i\) subordinate to the “rings” \(U_{i}:= V_{i+1}\setminus \overline{V_{i-1}}\) (the trickery with the indices is so that the \(U_i\) actually cover \(U\)). Where we relabel so that
\[
\begin{aligned}
\supp{\rho _i}\subset U_i .
\end{aligned}
\]
We have that \(\rho_i u \in C_c^\infty({V_{i+1}})\) with \(V_{i+1} \Subset U\) so by the local approximation in Theorem 3 we can find \(n_i\) such that
\[
\begin{aligned}
\norm{\phi_{n_i}*(\rho_i u)-\rho_i u}_{W^{k,p}(U)}=\norm{\phi_{n_i}*(\rho_i u)-\rho_i u}_{V_{i+1}}\leq \frac{\eps }{2^i} .
\end{aligned}
\]
Now we obtain the global approximation by taking
\[
\begin{aligned}
\varphi:= \sum_{i=1}^\infty \phi_{n_i}* (\rho_i u) \in C_{\mathrm{loc}}^\infty(U) .
\end{aligned}
\]
As then
\[
\begin{aligned}
\norm{\varphi-u}
_{W^{k,p}(U)}\leq\sum_{i=1}^\infty \norm{\phi_{n_i}*(\rho_i u)-\rho_i u }_{W^{k,p}(U)}\leq \sum_{i=1}^\infty \frac{\eps }{2^i}=\eps   .
\end{aligned}
\]

Note that Theorem 5 is not strictly stronger than Theorem 3 as it is not, in general, possible to extend functions in \(C^\infty_{\mathrm{loc}}(U)\) to \(C_c^\infty(\R^n)\). As a corollary of Meyers-Serrin’s theorem, we obtain an equivalent definition of \(W^{k,p}(U)\).

Exercise 16 Let \(U \subset \R^d\) be an open set and \(p<\infty\). Then \(W^{k,p}(U)\) is equal to the completion of \(C^\infty_{\mathrm{loc}}(U)\cap W^{k,p}(U)\) with the \(\norm{\cdot }_{W^{k,p}(U)}\) norm.







TipHint





Use that \(W^{k,p}(U)\) is complete and that the completion of a metric space is unique.



Before Theorem 5 was proved, both our original definition Definition 9 (distributions with derivatives in \(L^p(U)\)) and the one in Exercise 16 (closure of smooth functions with Sobolev norm) were used as the definition of \(W^{k,p}(U)\). But it was unclear which was the “correct” Sobolev space. This debate was settled by Meyers and Serrin who proved that, as we just showed, both are equal.
We now show an example of how these kinds of density results can be useful. The following generalizes the second point of Proposition 1

Exercise 17 (Change of variables) Let \(V,U\) be open in \(\R^d\) and \(\Phi: V \simeq U\) be bijective with \(\Phi \in C^k(V \to \R^d), \Phi^{-1} \in C^1(U\to \R^d)\). Then for any \(u \in  W^{k,p}(U)\) it holds that \(u \circ\Phi \in W^{k,p}(V)\) and the usual chain rule holds. For example
\[
\begin{aligned}
\partial _i (u\circ \Phi) = \sum_{j=1}^d (\partial _i \Phi_j)(\partial _j u_j)\circ \Phi_j  .
\end{aligned}
\tag{6}\]







TipHint





Give yourself an \(\eps\) of room. By induction, it suffices to consider the case \(k=1\).We can approximate \(u\) on each compact \(K \subset U\) by a sequence of functions \(u_n \in C_c^\infty(\R^d)\). For \(u_n\) the equality Equation 6 holds. Furthermore, by a change of variables, (6) is continuous in \(u\in W^{1,p}(\Omega)\) so we may pass to the limit and obtain (6) for \(u\) on \(K\). Since \(K\) was any, the equality also holds on the whole of \(U\).



In Exercise 17 it is important that \(\Phi\) is diffeomorphic so that composition with \(\Phi\) is continuous.

Exercise 18 Show that the conclusion of Exercise 17 is false if we only assume that \(\Phi \in C^k(\R^d)\) and do not impose invertibility.







TipHint





Divide by zero.



Now we provide a final global approximation result in the case where the domain is smooth (see Appendix Section 14 for a review on manifolds with boundary) and bounded.

Theorem 6 (Global, smooth on boundary approximation) Let \(\Omega\) be a bounded open domain with \(C^1\) boundary, then for all \(p<\infty\).
\[
\begin{aligned}
\overline{\restr{C_c^\infty(\R^d)}{\Omega}}= W^{k,p}(\Omega).
\end{aligned}
\]


Proof. The idea will be to locally translate points close to the boundary further into \(\Omega\) so that we can convolve with an approximation of unity and then recover the global case with a partition of unity. We begin by “straightening the boundary”. That is, since \(\partial \Omega\) is \(C^1\), given \(x_0 \in \partial  \Omega\) there exists an open set \(V \subset \R^d\) and a function \(\gamma \in  C^1(\R^{d-1})\) such that, relabeling and flipping the last coordinate axis if necessary
\[
\begin{aligned}
V \cap \Omega = \set{x \in V :x_d  >\gamma (x_1,\ldots,x_{d-1})}  .
\end{aligned}
\]
By considering a translation in the last coordinate \(e_d=(0,\ldots,0,1)\) and its mollification by an approximation of unity \(\phi_n\)
\[
\begin{aligned}
u_n(x):= u\qt{x+ \frac{2}{n}e_d }; \quad \varphi_n:= u_n *\phi_n .
\end{aligned}
\]
For \(n\) big enough we have that \(\varphi_n\) is well defined and smooth on \(W_0:=B_{\frac{1}{n}}(x_0)\). Since translation is continuous on \(L^p(U)\) for \(p \in [1,\infty)\) and by the behavior of differentiation with convolution (see Proposition 1), for \(n\) large enough
\[
\begin{aligned}
\norm{u -\varphi_n}_{W^{k,p}(W_0)}\leq \norm{u -u_n}_{W^{k,p}(W_0)}+\norm{u_n -u_n*\phi_n}_{W^{k,p}(W_0)}\leq \eps.
\end{aligned}
\]
Now, since \(\Omega\) is bounded \(\partial \Omega\) is compact we may extract a finite covering \(\set{W_i:=B_{\frac{1}{n_i}}(x_i)}_{i=0}^n\) of \(\partial \Omega\) and functions \(\set{\varphi_i}_{i=1}^n\) smooth on \(W_i\) such that
\[
\begin{aligned}
\norm{u -\varphi_n}_{W^{k,p}(W_i)}\leq \frac{\eps}{n+2}  .
\end{aligned}
\]
Now we take an open set \(W_{n+1} \Subset \Omega\) such that \(\set{W_i}_{i=0}^{n+1}\) cover \(\Omega\). By the local approximation of Theorem 3 we know we can approximate \(u\) on \(W_{n+1}\) by some \(\varphi_{n+1} \in C_c^\infty(\R^d)\)
\[
\begin{aligned}
\norm{u-\varphi_{n+1}}_{W^{k,p}(W_{n+1})}\leq\frac{\eps}{n+2} .
\end{aligned}
\]
Finally, we take a smooth partition of unity \(\set{\rho _i}_{i=0}^{n+1}\) subordinate to \(\set{W_i}_{i=0}^{n+1}\) and a bump function \(\eta \in C_c^\infty(\R^d)\) which is equal to \(1\) on \(\Omega\) and is supported on \(\bigcup_{i=0}^{n+1} W_i\) (see Example 5) and set
\[
\begin{aligned}
\varphi:= \eta \sum_{i=0}^{n+1} \rho _i \varphi_i \in  C^\infty_c(\Omega) .
\end{aligned}
\]
This gives the desired approximation
\[
\begin{aligned}
\norm{u-\varphi}_{W^{k,p}(\Omega)} \leq \sum_{i=0}^{n+1} \norm{\rho _i(u-\varphi_i)}_{W^{k,p}(W_i)} \leq  \eps  .
\end{aligned}
\]
This concludes the proof.

In contrast to Theorem 5, Theorem 6 shows that Sobolev functions on smooth bounded domains can be approximated by functions that are also smooth on the boundary of the domain \(\Omega\). This will prove fundamental in the next section. Both to extend them to the whole of \(\R^d\) and to restrict them to \(\partial \Omega\).

title: PDEs 3: Sobolev spaces
text:
8 Extensions and restrictions
Many results in the theory of Sobolev spaces are proved first on \(\R^d\) and then extended to more general domains by a continuous extension followed by a restriction. For example, if one can show that \(W^{k,p}(\R^d) \in L^q(\R^d)\) for some \(p,q,r\) then, denoting by \(\tilde{u}\) the extension of \(u\) to \(\R^d\)
\[
\begin{aligned}
\norm{u}_{L^q(\Omega)} \sim  \norm{u}_{L^q(\R^d)} \lesssim \norm{u}_{W^{k,p}(\R^d)} \sim \norm{u}_{W^{k,p}(\Omega)},
\end{aligned}
\]
which shows that \(W^{k,p}(\R^d) \subset L^q(\Omega)\). In the reasoning above, it is necessary for the extension to be continuous, otherwise we fail on the first \(\sim\). As we saw previously, merely extending by zero will not do and in this section, we develop the necessary tools to extend Sobolev functions from a domain \(\Omega\) to \(\R^d\).

Definition 10 Let \(X(U)\) be a topological space of functions for each open \(U \subset \R^d\). We say that \(\Omega\) is an extension domain for \(X\) if there exists a continuous operator
\[
\begin{aligned}
E: X(\Omega ) \to X(\R^d),
\end{aligned}
\]
such that \(Eu(x)=u(x)\) for almost all \(x \in \Omega\). We call \(E\) an extension operator.

For an extension to be possible, it is crucial that \(\Omega\) is regular enough. It will be sufficient to impose that \(\Omega\) have uniformly Lipschitz boundary. The following definition and Theorem can be found in (Leoni 2017, sec. 13).

Definition 11 We say that an open set \(\Omega \subset \R^d\) has uniformly Lipschitz boundary if there exists \(\eps,L>0, N \in \N\) and an open covering \(\set{U_n}_{n \in \N}\) of \(\partial \Omega\) such that

For all \(x \in \partial \Omega\) there exists a \(U_n\) such that \(B_\eps(x) \subset U_n\).
Every point in \(\R^d\) is contained in at most \(N\) sets \(U_n\).
For each \(n\) there exist local coordinates \(y=(y', y_n) \in \R^{d-1} \times \R\) and a Lipschitz continuous function \(f: \R^{d-1} \to \R\) (both depending on \(n\)), with Lipschitz constant \(\operatorname{Lip} f \leq L\), such that \(U_n \cap \Omega=U_n \cap V_n\), where \(V_n\) is given in local coordinates by
\[
\begin{aligned}
\set{(x', x_d) \in \R^{d-1} \times \R: x_d>f(x')} .
\end{aligned}
\]

That is, a domain is uniformly Lipschitz if it has a locally finite covering which makes it of type \(C^{0,1}\) (see Appendix Section 14). Below we show two typical examples.


Exercise 19 Show that if \(\Omega\) is bounded with Lipschitz boundary or if \(\Omega=\R^d\) then it has uniformly Lipschitz boundary.







TipHint





For the first part, use the fact that \(\Omega\) is bounded to extract a finite subcovering. For the second use that \(\partial\R^d=\emptyset\) and that everything is true for elements of the empty set.



The following theorem shows that if \(\partial\Omega\) is uniformly Lipschitz, \(\Omega\) is an extension domain for \(W^{k,p}\). To simplify the proof we will give the proof in the case of bounded open sets with \(C^k\) boundary. Using the approximation of Sobolev functions by functions smooth on the boundary and a change of variables, we can extend functions in \(W^{k,p}(\Omega)\) to the whole of \(\R^d\).

Theorem 7 (General extension) Let \(\Omega\subset \R^d\) be an open set with uniformly Lipschitz boundary. Then \(\Omega\) is an extension domain for \(W^{k,p}(\Omega)\) and \(C^k(\Omega)\) for all \(p \in [1,\infty]\).


Proof. We prove the result when the domain is bounded with \(C^k\) boundary and when \(p<\infty\). The general result can be found in (Leoni 2017, 424).
We work first in the upper half-space (the canonical example of a manifold with boundary)
\[
\begin{aligned}
\Hh^d =\set{x=(x_1,\ldots,x_d) \in \R^d : x_d \geq 0} .
\end{aligned}
\]
That is, we suppose that there is some open set \(\Omega' \subset  \R^d\) such that
\[
\begin{aligned}
\Omega=\Omega' \cap \Hh^d_{>0}.
\end{aligned}
\]
Where
\[
\begin{aligned}
\quad \Hh^d_{>0} =\mathrm{int}(\Hh^d)=\set{x=(x_1,\ldots,x_d) \in \R^d : x_d >0} .
\end{aligned}
\]
By Theorem 6 we also give ourselves an epsilon of room by supposing that \(u \in C^k(\overline{\Omega})\). We define the extension of \(u\) to \(\Omega'\) as
\[
\begin{aligned}
Eu(x) = \begin{cases}
u(x) \quad                                 & x_{d}                                 \geq 0      \\
\sum_{j=1}^{k+1} a_j  u_j(x - j e_d) \quad & x_d                                           < 0
\end{cases}.
\end{aligned}
\]
We will have \(Eu \in C^k(\Omega')\) as long as we can get the derivatives to match up on the boundary \(\{x_d=0\}\cap \overline{\Omega'}\). That is, as long as \(a_j\) verify
\[
\begin{aligned}
\sum_{j=1}^{k+1}(-j)^l a_j =1 , \quad l=0,1,\ldots,k .
\end{aligned}
\]
The above is a system of \(k+1\) equations with \(k+1\)-unknowns \(a_j\). Its matrix is the Vandermonde matrix (which is invertible). As a result, the system may be solved to extend \(u\). By the form of \(Eu\) we have the bound
\[
\begin{aligned}
\norm{Eu}_{W^{k,p}(\Omega')} \leq c\norm{u}_{W^{k,p}(\Omega)} .
\end{aligned}
\]
Where \(c:= (k+1)^{k+1}\max \set{1,\norm{a}_\infty}\). Working now in the general case for \(\Omega\), we may cover the compact \(\overline{\Omega}\) by a finite amount of bounded open sets \(\Omega_i \subset W\) such that
\[
\begin{aligned}
\Phi_i : \Omega_i \xrightarrow{\sim} \Omega_i'\quad \text{and}  \quad \Phi_i : \Omega_i \cap \Omega \xrightarrow{\sim} \Omega_i' \cap \Hh^d .
\end{aligned}
\]
Where \(\Omega_i'\) are open in \(\R^{d}\) and \(\Phi_i\) are \(C^k\) diffeomorphisms. By the previous case, we can extend \(u'_i:=u \circ \Phi_i^{-1} \in C^k (\Omega_i' \cap \Hh^d)\) to functions \(\widetilde{u'_i} \in C^k(\Omega_i')\) and then transform back to the original space to get
\[
\begin{aligned}
\tilde{u_i}:= \widetilde{u'_i} \circ \Phi_i \in C^k(\Omega_i).
\end{aligned}
\]
Where
\[
\begin{aligned}
\norm{\tilde{u_i}}_{W^{k,p}(\Omega_i)}\lesssim  \norm{u_i}_{W^{k,p}(\Omega)}.
\end{aligned}
\tag{7}\]
The hidden constant depending only on \(c,\norm{\Phi_i}_{C^k(\Omega_i)}\norm{\Phi_i^{-1}}_{C^k(\Omega_i')}\). Next, using a partition of unity subordinate to \(\set{\Omega_i}_{i=1}^n\) we glue the local extensions together to form an extension to all of \(\Omega\)
\[
\begin{aligned}
\tilde{u}:= \sum_{i=1}^n \rho _i{u_i} \in C^k(\Omega).
\end{aligned}
\]
The desired extension can now be obtained by multiplying \(\tilde{u}\) with a bump function \(\eta\) that is supported in \(W\) and equal to \(1\) on \(\Omega\).
\[
\begin{aligned}
Eu := \eta \tilde{u} \in C^k(\R^d).
\end{aligned}
\]
By (7) we have that
\[
\begin{aligned}
\norm{E u}_{W^{k,p}(\R^d)} \lesssim  \norm{u}_{W^{k,p}(\Omega)}.
\end{aligned}
\]
As a result, \(E\) is a (bounded) linear operator. So far we had considered \(u \in  C^k(\overline{\Omega})\). Now, since by Theorem 6 the space \(C^k(\overline{\Omega})\) is dense in \(W^{k,p}(\Omega)\) we may extend \(E\) to a linear operator on the whole of \(W^{k,p}(\Omega)\). A verification shows that \(E\) is also bounded as an operator from \(C^k(\Omega)\) to \(C^k(\R^d)\). This concludes the proof.


Exercise 20 (Restriction) Under the conditions of ?@thm-extension it holds that
\[
\begin{aligned}
W^{k,p}(\Omega)= \restr{W^{k,p}(\R^d)}{\Omega}; \quad C^{k}(\Omega)= \restr{C^{k}(\R^d)}{\Omega}.
\end{aligned}
\]
That is, functions in \(W^{k,p}(\Omega),C^{k}(\Omega)\) are equal to the restriction of functions in \(W^{k,p}(\R^d),C^{k}(\R^d)\) respectively.







TipHint





Given \(u \in W^{k,p}(\Omega)\) we can extend it to \(Eu \in W^{k,p}(\R^d)\) by the just proved extension ?@thm-extension. By definition \(u= \restr{Eu}{\Omega}\). The case \(u \in C^k(\R^d)\) is identical.



Using extensions also gives us a way to define the Sobolev spaces \(H^s(\Omega)\) when the exponent \(s\) is real valued.

Definition 12 Given a bounded open set \(\Omega\) with boundary of type \(C^k\) with \(k \in \N_+\), we define for all real \(s \in [0,k]\)
\[
\begin{aligned}
H^s(\Omega):=\restr{H^s(\R^d)}{\Omega} .
\end{aligned}
\]

To further generalize this definition to domains where restriction is not possible one needs to use complex interpolation (see for example (Taylor 2013) pages 321-333).

title: PDEs 3: Sobolev spaces
text:
9 Trace theorem
As we already discussed, a PDE often incorporates boundary information such as \(\restr{u}{\partial \Omega}=0\). This is well defined if \(u\) is continuous, however, if \(u \in  W^{k,p}(U)\), and is thus only defined almost everywhere, then \(\restr{u}{\partial  U}\) is a priori not well defined. The following theorem remedies this issue.

Proposition 3 (Trace) Let \(\Omega \subset \R^d\) be open with Lipschitz boundary and let \(p\in[1,\infty)\). Then there exists a continuous linear operator
\[
\begin{aligned}
\operatorname{Tr}: W^{1,p}(\Omega)\to L^p_{\mathrm{loc}}(\partial \Omega) .
\end{aligned}
\]
Such that \(\operatorname{Tr}(u) =\restr{u}{\partial \Omega}\) for all \(u \in C(\overline{\Omega}) \cap W^{1,p}(\Omega)\).


Proof. We will only prove the case where \(\Omega\) is bounded and of class \(C^1\). The proof of the general statement can once more be found in (Leoni 2017, 592).
As in previous results, the trick is to suppose first \(u\) is smooth, work locally, and then obtain a global result using a partition of unity and the density in Theorem 6. \
Given \(x_0 \in \partial  \Omega\) we take a open set \(U \subset \R^d\) containing \(x_0\). Flattening out the boundary by \(\Phi : U \simeq U'\) where necessarily the boundary is preserved
\[
\begin{aligned}
\Phi: U \cap  \partial \Omega\iso U' \cap \partial  \Hh^d,
\end{aligned}
\]
and using the extension ?@thm-extension to extend \(u':= u\circ \Phi\) to \(\widetilde{u}'\) with compact support \(K \subset \R^d\) we obtain by the <https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Divergence_theorem#:~:text=space
\[
\begin{aligned}
\int_{U'\cap \partial \Hh^d}\abs{u'}^p  \leq\int_{\partial \Hh^d} \abs{\widetilde{u}'}^p=\int_{\Hh^d} \partial_{d} \abs{\widetilde{u}'}^p\leq\int_{\Hh^d} p\abs{\widetilde{u}'}^{p-1}\abs{\partial_{d} u}   \lesssim \norm{\widetilde{u}'}_{W^{1,p}(\Hh^d)}^p  .
\end{aligned}
\]
Where in the second inequality we used the chain rule and in the last Hölder’s inequality. Since \(\Phi^{-1}\) is \(C^k\) and by the continuity of the extension we obtain what we are looking for in
\[
\begin{aligned}
\norm{u}_{L^p(U \cap \partial \Hh^d)}\lesssim \norm{\widetilde{u}'}_{W^{1,p}(\Hh^d)}\lesssim \norm{u'}_{W^{1,p}(U'\cap \Hh^d)}\lesssim  \norm{u}_{W^{1,p}(U\cap \Omega)}
\end{aligned}
\tag{8}\]
We had supposed \(u\) smooth, now taking a finite covering \(\set{U_i}_{i=1}^n\) of \(\partial  \Omega\) (this is possible by compactness of \(\partial  \Omega\)) and taking a subordinate partition of unity \(\rho _i\) we conclude from (8) that
\[
\begin{aligned}
\int_{\partial \Omega} \abs{u}^p  =\sum_{i=1}^n \int_{ U_i \cap \partial \Omega} \abs{u}^p \lesssim \sum_{i=1}^n \norm{u}_{W^{1,p}(U_i \cap \Omega)}^p =\norm{u}_{W^{1,p}(U)}^p .
\end{aligned}
\]
That is,
\[
\begin{aligned}
\norm{\operatorname{Tr}(u)}_{L^p( \partial \Omega)}\lesssim  \norm{u}_{W^{1,p}(U)} .
\end{aligned}
\]
Using Theorem 6 to extend \(\operatorname{Tr}\) continuously to \(W^{k,p}(\Omega)= \overline{\restr{C_c^\infty(\R^d)}{\Omega}}\) concludes the proof.

Note that, if \(\Omega\) is bounded, then \(L^p_{\mathrm{loc}}(\partial \Omega)=L^p(\partial \Omega)\) so \(\operatorname{Tr}(u) \in L^p(\partial \Omega)\).

Definition 13 We define the trace of \(u \in W^{k,p}(\Omega)\) as \(\operatorname{Tr}(u)\). We also use the notation
\[
\begin{aligned}
\restr{u}{\partial \Omega}:= \operatorname{Tr}(u).
\end{aligned}
\]

To get an estimate on the trace we paid \(1\)-degree of regularity. We can do better and only pay \({1}/{p}\) degrees of regularity. This uses the theory of <https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Trace_operator#For_p_=_1:~:text=

Theorem 8 For all real \(s> 1 /2\) the trace operator is a continuous operator
\[
\begin{aligned}
\operatorname{Tr}: H^s(\Omega) \to H^{s -\frac{1}{2}}(\Omega).
\end{aligned}
\]


Proof. Straightening out the boundary and using the extension operator as in the trace Proposition 3, it is sufficient to work in the case where \(u\) is smooth and defined on \(\Hh^{d}\). By Fourier inversion, if we write \(\xi=(\xi',\xi _d)\)
\[
\begin{aligned}
\wh{\operatorname{Tr}(u)}(\xi ')=\int_{\R}\wh{u}(\xi) \d \xi _d .
\end{aligned}
\]
So, by Cauchy-Schwarz
\[
\begin{aligned}
\abs{\wh{\operatorname{Tr}(u)}(\xi ')}^2\leq \int_{\R}\abs{\wh{u}(\xi)}^2\br{\xi }^{2s} \d \xi _d \int_{\R}\br{\xi }^{-2s} \d \xi _d.
\end{aligned}
\tag{9}\]
The change of variables \(\xi _d \to \br{\xi '}\xi _d\) shows that
\[
\begin{aligned}
\int_{\R}\br{\xi }^{-2s} \d \xi _d=\br{\xi' }^{-2(s-\frac{1}{2})} \int_{\R}\br{\xi _d}^{-2s}\d \xi _d\lesssim  \br{\xi' }^{-2(s-\frac{1}{2})}
\end{aligned}
\tag{10}\]
Where in the inequality it was used that \(s> 1 /2\). We deduce from (9) and (10) on taking norms that
\[
\begin{aligned}
\norm{u}_{H^{s-1 /2}(\R^{d-1})} \lesssim  \norm{u}_{H^s(\R^d)}.
\end{aligned}
\]
Which concludes the proof.

A particularly useful space of functions related to the trace operator is the following

Definition 14 We define the space of functions with trace zero as
\[
\begin{aligned}
W^{k,p}_0(U):=\overline{C_c^\infty(U)}\subset W^{k,p}(U).
\end{aligned}
\]
Where the closure is with respect to the topology on \(W^{k,p}(U)\).


Exercise 21 Show that for all \(u \in W^{k,p}_0(\Omega)\)
\[
\begin{aligned}
\restr{u}{\partial \Omega}=0.
\end{aligned}
\]







TipHint





Use the continuity of the trace operator.



The converse to Exercise 21 holds but is far from trivial.

Proposition 4 Let \(\Omega\) be a bounded open set with \(C^1\) boundary. Then
\[
\begin{aligned}
W^{k,p}_0(\Omega)=\set{u \in W^{k,p}(\Omega): \operatorname{Tr}(u)=0} .
\end{aligned}
\]

The proof is very technical, see (Evans 2022) page 274 for the details.\
Being able to approximate functions in \(W_0^{k,p}(U)\) by smooth functions compactly supported inside of \(U\) gives us many more tools. For example, a function \(u\) in \(W^{k,p}_0(U)\) can be extended by zero to obtain an element \(\tl{u}\) in \(W^{k,p}(\R^d)\) even for non-smooth unbounded domains.

Exercise 22 (Extension trace 0) Let \(U\) be an open set, and define
\[
\begin{aligned}
\widetilde{u}:=\begin{cases}
u & \text{ on } U   \\
0 & \text{ on } U^c
\end{cases}.
\end{aligned}
\]
Then
\[
\begin{aligned}
E: W_0^{k,p}(U) \to W^{k,p}(\R^d); \quad  u \to \widetilde{u}
\end{aligned}
\]
is a linear with \(\norm{E}=1\).







TipHint





It is immediate that \(\norm{\widetilde{u}}_{W^{k,p}(\R^d)}=\norm{\widetilde{u}}_{W^{k,p}(U)}\) for \(u \in C_c^\infty(U)\). As a result, we can extend \(E\) by density to the closure \(C_c^\infty(U)\) in \(W^{k,p}(U)\). Which by definition is \(W^{k,p}_0(U)\).




Remark 4. The fact that we can extend functions in \(W^{k,p}_0(U)\) for arbitrary \(U\) allows one to derive results that when stated for the whole of \(W^{k,p}(U)\) require \(U\) to be smooth so that it is possible to extend \(U\).


Exercise 23 (Integration by parts 1) Let \(U\) be any open set and consider \(u \in  W^{1,p}_0(U), v \in W^{1,p'}(U)\) then
\[
\begin{aligned}
\int_{U} (\partial _iu) v = -\int_{U}u \partial _i v  .
\end{aligned}
\]







TipHint





Give yourself an epsilon of room and take limits.




Exercise 24 (Integration by parts 2) Let \(\Omega\) be a bounded open set of \(\R^d\) with \(C^1\) boundary and consider \(u \in  W^{1,p}(\Omega), v \in W^{1,p'}(\Omega)\) then
\[
\begin{aligned}
\int_{\Omega} (\partial _iu) v = -\int_{\Omega}u \partial _i v+ \int_{\partial \Omega} u v **n**_i .
\end{aligned}
\]
Where \(**n**\) is the outward pointing unit normal vector to \(\partial \Omega\).







TipHint





Give yourself an epsilon of room, apply the <https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Divergence_theorem#:~:text=space

title: PDEs 3: Sobolev spaces
text:
10 Sobolev embeddings and inequalities
Sobolev inequalities are relationships that bound the norm of \(u\) in different function spaces depending on how differentiable and integrable \(u\) is. For example, such a relationship could look like
\[
\begin{aligned}
\norm{u}_{W^{ l,p^* }(\Omega)}\leq \norm{u}_{W^{k,p}(\Omega)}.
\end{aligned}
\tag{11}\]
By considering the rescaling \(u(\lambda x)\), performing a change of variables, and taking \(\lambda\) to \(\infty\) we see that for such a relationship to hold it is necessary that
\[
\begin{aligned}
l-     \frac{d}{p^*}=k-\frac{d}{p}  .
\end{aligned}
\tag{12}\]
The case \(k=l+1\) gives rise to the following definition

Definition 15 The Sobolev conjugate  of \(1\leq p<d\) is \(p^*\) defined by
\[
\begin{aligned}
\frac{1}{p^*}= \frac{1}{p} -\frac{1}{d} .
\end{aligned}
\]

Note that \(p<p^*\). The idea behind inequalities such as (11) is to cash in some differentiability for some integrability. The main results used to do this are based on the fundamental theorem of calculus. First, we need the following lemma.

Lemma 2 (Loomis-Whitney inequality) Let \(d \geq 1\), let \(f_1, \ldots, f_d \in L^p\left(\mathbb{R}^{d-1}\right)\) for some \(p \in (0,\infty]\), and define
\[
\begin{aligned}
F_d\left(x_1, \ldots, x_d\right):=\prod_{i=1}^d f_i\left(x_1, \ldots, x_{i-1}, x_{i+1}, \ldots, x_d\right).
\end{aligned}
\]
Then,
\[
\begin{aligned}
\norm{F_d}_{L^{p/(d-1)}}\leq\prod_{i=1}^d\left\|f_i\right\|_{L^p\left(\mathbb{R}^{d-1}\right)} .
\end{aligned}
\tag{13}\]


Proof. The case \(d=2\) is immediate by Fubini. The general case follows from induction on \(d\) . We write \((x_1,\ldots x_{d+1})=(x',x_{d+1})\)
\[
\begin{aligned}
& \norm{F_{d+1}}_{L^{p /d}(\R^{d+1})}  =\qt{\int_{\R}\qt{\int_{\R^d} F_d(x)^{\frac{p}{d}} f_{d+1}(x')^{\frac{p}{d}} \d x'  }  \d x_{d+1} }^{\frac{d}{p} }          \\
& \leq \qt{\int_{\R}         \qt{\int_{\R^d} F_d(x)^{\frac{p}{d-1}} \d x'  }^{\frac{d-1}{d}}\d x_{d+1} }^{\frac{d}{p} }\norm{f_{d+1}}_{L^p(\R^d)}      \notag      \\
& \leq \qt{\int_{\R} \prod_{i=1}^d\left\|f_i(x_{d+1})\right\|_{L^p\left(\mathbb{R}^{d}\right)}^{\frac{p}{d}} \d x_{d+1}}^{\frac{d}{p}}\norm{f_{d+1}}_{L^p(\R^{d})}
\end{aligned}
\tag{14}\]
Where in the first inequality we applied Cauchy-Schwarz with \(q= d /(d-1), q' =d\). Now applying the general version of Hölder’s inequality \[
\begin{aligned}
\norm{g_1\cdots g_n }_{L^1} \leq \norm{g_1}_{L^{p_1}}\cdots\norm{g_1}_{L^{p_n}}; \quad  \frac{1}{p_1}+\cdots \frac{1}{p_n}=1\notag          .
\end{aligned}
\]
to \(g_i:= \norm{f_i(\cdot )}^{\frac{p}{d}}_{L^p(\R^{d})} \in L^{d}(\R)\) gives
\[
\begin{aligned}
\int_{\R}\prod_{i=1}^{d}g_i \leq \prod_{i=1}^{d} \norm{g_i}_{L^d(\R)}    .
\end{aligned}
\]
Substituting into (14) concludes the proof.

Using the Loomis-Whitney inequality and the fundamental theorem of calculus gives us our first Sobolev inequality

Theorem 9 (Sobolev-Gagliardo-Niremberg) Given \(1 \leq p<d\) it holds that
\[
\begin{aligned}
\norm{u}_{L^{p^*}(\R^d)}\lesssim  \norm{\nabla u}_{L^p (\R^d)}.
\end{aligned}
\]


Proof. By density (see Theorem 4), it is enough to take \(u \in C_c^\infty(\R^d)\). Applying the fundamental theorem of calculus gives
\[
\begin{aligned}
\abs{u}^m=\int_{-\infty}^\cdot \partial _i \abs{u}^m \d x_i  \leq m\int_{\R}\abs{u}^{m-1}\abs{\partial _i u} \d x_i=: f_i , \quad\forall i=1,\ldots,d.
\end{aligned}
\]
Multiplying all these inequalities together gives
\[
\begin{aligned}
\abs{u}^{md} \leq \prod_{i=1}^{d} f_i   .
\end{aligned}
\]
Applying the Loomis-Whitney inequality (13) “with \(p=1\)” and Hölder’s inequality shows
\[
\begin{aligned}
\norm{u}_{L^{ md /(d-1)}(\R^d)}^{md/(d-1)}\lesssim  \prod_{i=1}^{d}  \norm{u^{m-1}\partial_iu}_{L^1(\R^d)} \leq \norm{u}_{L^{(m-1)p'}(\R^d)}^{(m-1)d}\norm{\nabla u}_{L^{p}(\R^d \to  \R^d)}^d
\end{aligned}
\tag{15}\]
It remains to choose \(m\) such that
\[
\begin{aligned}
\frac{md}{d-1}= (m-1)p' \implies m= \frac{(d-1)p^*}{d} =\frac{dp -p}{d-p} \geq 1.
\end{aligned}
\]
Substituting into (15) gives
\[
\begin{aligned}
\norm{u}_{L^{ p^*}(\R^d)}^{m}\lesssim \norm{u}_{L^{p^*}(\R^d)}^{m-1}\norm{\nabla u}_{L^{p}(\R^d \to  \R^d)} .
\end{aligned}
\]
This concludes the proof.


Exercise 25 Given \(p<\frac{d}{k}\) define \(p_k^*\) by \(\frac{1}{p_k^*}=\frac{1}{p}-\frac{k}{d}\). Then,
\[
\begin{aligned}
\norm{u}_{L^{p_k^*}(\R^d)} \lesssim  \norm{\nabla^k u}_{L^p(\R^d)}
\end{aligned}
\]







TipHint





Apply induction on \(k\) using Theorem 9.



The result can also be further <https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Gagliardo An estimate for the endpoint \(d=p\) can also be achieved

Theorem 10 It holds that
\[
\begin{aligned}
W^{1,d}(\R^d) \hookrightarrow L^q(\R^d) , \quad\forall  q \in [d,+\infty)
\end{aligned}
\]


Proof. Setting \(p=d\) in our estimate (15) gives
\[
\begin{aligned}
\norm{u}_{L^{ m d/(d-1)}(\R^d)}^{m}\lesssim  \norm{u}_{L^{(m-1)d /(d-1)}(\R^d)}^{m-1}\norm{\nabla u}_{L^{d}(\R^d \to  \R^d)} .
\end{aligned}
\]
Applying Young’s product inequality with \(p = \frac{m}{m-1}, p'= m\) and using that raising to a power is convex gives
\[
\begin{aligned}
\norm{u}_{L^{ m d/(d-1)}(\R^d)}\lesssim \norm{u}_{L^{(m-1)d/(d-1)}(\R^d)}+\norm{\nabla u}_{L^{d}(\R^d \to  \R^d)} , \quad\forall  m \geq 1.
\end{aligned}
\tag{16}\]
Taking \(m=d\) above gives
\[
\begin{aligned}
\norm{u}_{L^{d^2 /(d-1)}(\R^d)} \lesssim  \norm{u}_{W^{1,d}(\R^d)}.
\end{aligned}
\]
We also trivially have \(\norm{u}_{L^d(\R^d)} \leq\norm{u}_{W^{1,d}(\R^d)}\) so by <https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Riesz
\[
\begin{aligned}
\norm{u}_{L^{q}(\R^d)} \lesssim  \norm{u}_{W^{1,d}(\R^d)} , \quad\forall q \in \qb{d,\frac{d^2}{d-1}}.
\end{aligned}
\tag{17}\]
We now iterate, taking \(m=d+1\) gives
\[
\begin{aligned}
\norm{u}_{L^{ (d+1)d/(d-1)}(\R^d)}\lesssim \norm{u}_{L^{d^2/(d-1)}(\R^d)}+\norm{\nabla u}_{L^{d}(\R^d \to  \R^d)}
\end{aligned}
\]
Which combined with (17) and interpolating gives
\[
\begin{aligned}
\norm{u}_{L^{q}(\R^d)} \lesssim  \norm{u}_{W^{1,d}(\R^d)} , \quad\forall q \in \qb{d,\frac{(d+1)d}{d-1}}.
\end{aligned}
\]
Iterating this process (taking \(m=d+2,\ldots, m=d+k\) in (16)) shows that
\[
\begin{aligned}
\norm{u}_{L^{q}(\R^d)} \lesssim  \norm{u}_{W^{1,d}(\R^d)} , \quad\forall q \in \qb{d+k,\frac{(d+k)d}{d-1}}.
\end{aligned}
\]
From this, we conclude the result.


Exercise 26 It holds that
\[
\begin{aligned}
W^{k,\frac{d}{k}}(\R^d) \hookrightarrow L^q(\R^d) , \quad\forall  q \in [d /k,+\infty)
\end{aligned}
\]







TipHint





Use induction on \(k\) with Theorem 10.



Note that the constant in our above estimate blows up on iterating. As a result, we do not expect
\[
\begin{aligned}
W^{1,d}(\R^d) \hookrightarrow L^\infty(\R^d).
\end{aligned}
\]
This holds if and only if \(d=1\). Otherwise, we require more integrability. However, this extra integrability can be converted into regularity in the converse direction of Sobolev spaces. First, we recall the following definition

Definition 16 Let \(U \subset \R^d\) be open and \(\gamma \in \R_+\), we define the Hölder space
\[
\begin{aligned}
C^{k,\gamma }(\R^d):= \set{u \in C^k(U): \norm{u}_{C^{k,\gamma }(U)}<\infty} .
\end{aligned}
\]
Where the Holder norm is defined as
\[
\begin{aligned}
\norm{u}_{C^{0,\gamma }(U)} & := \sup_{x \neq y \in \R^d} \frac{\abs{u(x)-u(y)} }{\abs{x-y}^\gamma  }                \\
\norm{u}_{C^{k,\gamma }(U)} & :=\norm{u}_{C^{k}(U)}+\sum_{\abs{\alpha}= k } \norm{D^\alpha u}_{C^{0,\gamma }(U)}   .
\end{aligned}
\]

For \(\gamma =1\) the \(C^{k,\gamma }\) is the space of functions with bounded derivatives up to order \(k\) and whose \(k\)-th order derivatives are Lipschitz continuous.

Theorem 11 (Morrey) Let \(p>d\) and set \(\gamma=1-\frac{d}{p}\). Then, the following inclusion is continuous
\[
\begin{aligned}
W^{1,p}(\R^d) \hookrightarrow C^{0,\gamma }(\R^d).
\end{aligned}
\]

The proof is technical and can be found in (Brezis and Brézis 2011) page 282.
As is logical, as \(p\) approaches \(d\) from above the extra differentiability we get goes to \(0\). Furthermore, no matter how much integrability we cash in, we can never get more differentiability than we started with, so \(\gamma \to 1\) as \(p \to \infty\).

Exercise 27 (Sobolev regularity) Let \(kp>d\) and \(\frac{d}{p} \notin \N\). Set \(m = k - \lfloor d/p \rfloor - 1\) and \(\gamma = \lfloor d/p \rfloor + 1 -\frac{d}{p}\). Then, the following inclusion is continuous
\[
\begin{aligned}
W^{k,p}(\R^d) \hookrightarrow C^{m,\gamma }(\R^d).
\end{aligned}
\]







TipHint





By iteratively applying the continuous Sobolev embedding \(l=\lfloor d/p \rfloor\) times, we obtain a chain of continuous inclusions: \[W^{k,p}(\R^d) \hookrightarrow W^{k-1, p_1}(\R^d) \hookrightarrow \dots \hookrightarrow W^{k-l, p_l}(\R^d),\] where the exponents are governed by the relation \[\frac{1}{p_j} = \frac{1}{p_{j-1}} - \frac{1}{d} \implies \frac{1}{p_l} = \frac{1}{p} - \frac{l}{d}.\] So by assumption we verify the Morrey Condition \(p_l > d\). As a result, \(W^{1, p_l}(\R^d) \hookrightarrow C^{0, \gamma}(\R^d)\) with \(\gamma = 1 - \frac{d}{p_l} = \lfloor d/p \rfloor + 1 - \frac{d}{p}\).
We started with \(k\) derivatives, spent \(l\) to reach integrability \(p_l\), and \(1\) to apply Morrey’s inequality and obtain \(C^{0, \gamma}\) regularity. The number of classical derivatives remaining is \(m = k - l - 1 = k - \lfloor d/p \rfloor - 1\).
In the edge case where \(\frac{d}{p} = l\) is an integer, the bootstrapping argument stops at \(W^{1,d}(\R^d)\), which embeds into \(L^q(\R^d)\) for all \(q<\infty\), but fails to embed into \(L^\infty(\R^d)\) and \(C^{0,\gamma}\). To guarantee continuity, we must sacrifice to a slightly lower integrability \(q_\eps = d-\eps < d\) in our final bootstrapping step. This pushes us back into the Morrey regime where \(p_{l-1} > d\), yielding \(\gamma = 1 - \frac{d}{q_\eps} \in (0,1)\), meaning we achieve \(C^{m, \gamma}(\R^d)\) for all \(\gamma \in (0,1)\).



Combining the three results gives

Theorem 12 (Rellich-Kondrachov) Let \(\Omega\) be bounded with \(C^k\) boundary, then the following inclusions are continuous
\[
\begin{aligned}
