& W^{k,p}(\Omega) \hookrightarrow L^q(\Omega) ,           \quad\forall q \in [1,p_k^*]                              & \text{ and }     p<\frac{d}{k} \\
& W^{k,p}(\Omega) \hookrightarrow L^q(\Omega) ,           \quad\forall q \in [p,\infty)                              & \text{ and }   p=\frac{d}{k}   \\
& W^{k,p}(\Omega) \hookrightarrow C^{m,\gamma }(\overline{\Omega}) \hookrightarrow C^{m}(\overline{\Omega}) ,  \quad & \text{ and }  p>\frac{d}{k}
\end{aligned}
\]
Where \(p_k^*\) is defined by the relation \(\frac{1}{p_k^*}=\frac{1}{p}-\frac{k}{d}\), \(m = k - \lfloor d/p \rfloor - 1\), and \(\gamma = \lfloor d/p \rfloor + 1 -\frac{d}{p}\) or \(\gamma \in (0,1)\) if \(\frac{d}{p} \in \mathbb{N}\). Furthermore, the first for \(q<p_k^*\), second, fourth, and third composed with fourth inclusions are compact.


Exercise 28 Show that if \(\Omega\) is unbounded then the inclusions in Theorem 12 are not compact.







TipHint





Consider a sequence of smooth functions whose support goes to infinity. For example, for \(\Omega = \R^d\) consider the sequence \(u_n(x) = \varphi(x-n)\) where \(\varphi\in C^\infty_c(\R^d)\).




Proof. The fact that the above inclusions are continuous follows by our three Sobolev inequalities in Exercises Exercise 25,Exercise 26,Exercise 27 together with the extension ?@thm-extension.
The compactness of the embeddings requires reduce to showing that the unit ball in each of the embedded spaces is equicontinuous.

For the first inclusion we will use <https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fr
\[
\begin{aligned}
\norm{\tau_h u-u}_{L^p(\Omega)}\leq \norm{\nabla u}_{L^p(\Omega)}\abs{h} , \quad\forall u \in W^{1,p}(\Omega) .
\end{aligned}
\]
Where the above is known to be true for smooth functions by the fundamental theorem of calculus and extends by density to \(W^{k,p}(\Omega)\) (we recall translation is continuous on \(L^p\) for \(p<\infty\)). The above also holds for \(p_k^*\) and since \(p<q<p_k^*\) we can write
\[
\begin{aligned}
\frac{1}{q}=\frac{\alpha}{p}+\frac{1-\alpha}{p_k^*}   .
\end{aligned}
\]
By <https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Riesz
\[
\begin{aligned}
\norm{\tau_h u-u}_{L^q(\Omega)}\leq \norm{\nabla u}_{L^p(\Omega)}^\alpha\norm{\nabla u}_{L^{p_k^*}(\Omega)}^{1-\alpha}\abs{h} , \quad\forall u \in W^{1,p}(\Omega) .
\end{aligned}
\]
This shows equicontinuity and concludes the first case.
The compactness of the second inclusion is proved identically.
For the compactness of the last inclusion we use <https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Arzel
\[
\begin{aligned}
\abs{D^\alpha u(x)- D^\alpha u(y)} & \lesssim  \norm{u}_{C^{m}(\R^d)}\abs{x-y} ,                 &  & \quad\forall \abs{\alpha}<m  \\
\abs{D^\alpha u(x)- D^\alpha u(y)} & \lesssim  \norm{u}_{C^{m,\gamma }(\R^d)}\abs{x-y}^\gamma  , &  & \quad\forall \abs{\alpha}=m.
\end{aligned}
\]
As a result, for each \(\abs{\alpha}\leq m\) the family
\[
\begin{aligned}
A_\alpha:=\set{D^\alpha u: u \in B_1 \subset C^{m,\gamma }(\overline{\Omega})},
\end{aligned}
\]
is equicontinuous. So by Arzelà–Ascoli we may extract a sequence \(u_{n}\) such that \(D^\alpha u_n\) converges uniformly to some \(u^{(\alpha)}\). By the fundamental theorem of calculus we conclude that \(u^{(\alpha)}=D^\alpha u^{(0)}\) and as a result \(u_n \to u \in C^m(\overline{\Omega})\). That is, the unit ball \(B_1 \subset C^{m,\gamma }\) is sequentially compact and thus compact when embedded in \(C^m(\overline{\Omega})\). This proves this point.
The composition of the inclusions on the last line is compact as the composition of a compact and a continuous operator is compact. This concludes the proof.


As a particular case of the above we obtain the following simpler looking corollary

Corollary 1 Let \(q< p_k^*\), then it holds that following inclusion is compact
\[
\begin{aligned}
W^{k,p}(\Omega) \hookrightarrow L^q(\Omega).
\end{aligned}
\]
In particular, for all \(p \in [1, \infty]\), the following inclusion is compact
\[
\begin{aligned}
W^{k,p}(\Omega) \hookrightarrow L^p(\Omega).
\end{aligned}
\]


Proof. For any of the cases in Rellich-Kondrachov’s Theorem 12, the condition on \(q\) holds. The first part of the result then follows as \(C^k(\overline{\Omega}) \hookrightarrow L^q(\Omega)\) and the second part from the fact that \(p< p^{k^*}\).

Compact embeddings in spaces of higher order can be obtained through the following.

Proposition 5 (Higher degree embeddings) Let \(k,l \in \N_0\) and \(p,q \in [1,\infty]\) be such that
\[
\begin{aligned}
W^{k,p}(\Omega)\hookrightarrow L^q(\Omega)
\end{aligned}
\]
is compact. Then, the inclusion
\[
\begin{aligned}
W^{k+l,p}(\Omega)\hookrightarrow W^{l,q}(\Omega)
\end{aligned}
\]
is also compact.


Proof. Let \(u_n \in W^{k+l,p}(U)\) be a bounded sequence. By assumption \(W^{k,p}(\Omega)\) is compact in \(L^q(\Omega)\), so we may extract a subsequence \(u_{n_k}\) such that, for \(\abs{\alpha} \leq l\),
\[
\begin{aligned}
\lim_{k \to \infty}D^\alpha u_{n_k} = u^{(\alpha)} \in L^q(\Omega) .
\end{aligned}
\]
Using the same technique as in Theorem 2 shows that \(u^{(\alpha)}= D^{\alpha}u\). This shows that \(u_{n_k} \to u\) in \(W^{l,q}(\Omega)\) and, by the equivalence of compactness and sequential compactness, concludes the proof.

This result can be combined directly with Rellich-Kondrachov Theorem 12 or any other embedding. For example, when combined with Corollary 1 we get

Corollary 2 Let \(q< p_k^*\), then it holds that following inclusion is compact
\[
\begin{aligned}
W^{k+l,p}(\Omega) \hookrightarrow W^{l,q}(\Omega).
\end{aligned}
\]
In particular, for all \(p \in [1, \infty]\), the following inclusion is compact
\[
\begin{aligned}
W^{k+l,p}(\Omega) \hookrightarrow W^{l,p}(\Omega).
\end{aligned}
\]

Using Remark 4 we can also obtain Sobolev embeddings for non-smooth domains if we consider Sobolev spaces with trace \(0\).

Theorem 13 (Rellich for trace 0 and irregular domain) Let \(U\subset \R^d\) be a bounded domain. Then, the conclusions of Theorem 12, higher degree embeddings, and its corollary hold if we swap everywhere \(W^{k,p}(\Omega)\) for \(W^{k,p}_0(U)\) and \(W^{k+l,p}(\Omega)\) for \(W^{k+l,p}_0(U)\).


Proof. The proof follows in an identical method as the aforementioned results as we still have density of smooth functions \(W^{k,p}_0(U)\) and the extension theorem, which is now given by Exercise 22.

The main utility of all these compact embeddings is that given a sequence \(u_n\) whose derivatives are bounded in certain \(L^p\) norms we can extract convergent subsequences in appropriate spaces. We end this post (modulo appendices) with one of the most useful inequalities which we will make use of in future posts.

Theorem 14 (Poincaré inequality) Let \(u \in W^{1,p}_0(U)\) where \(U\) is bounded in one direction. Then
\[
\begin{aligned}
\norm{u}_{L^p(U)}\leq C\norm{\nabla u}_{L^p(U \to \R^d)}.
\end{aligned}
\]


Proof. By density (which holds by definition of \(W^{k,p}_0(U)\)) of it is sufficient to reason for \(u \in C_c^\infty(U)\) and pass to the limit. By relabeling we may suppose \(U\) is bounded along the \(x_d\) axis. That is, for some finite \(a<b\)
\[
\begin{aligned}
U \subset \R^{d-1} \times [a,b].
\end{aligned}
\]
The idea is to use the fundamental theorem of calculus together with the compact support of \(u\). This allows us to rewrite
\[
\begin{aligned}
\abs{u(x)}= \abs{\int_{a}^{x_d} \partial_d u(x',t) \d t} \leq \qt{\int_{\R}\abs{\partial _d u(x',t)}^p \d t}^\frac{1}{p}(x_d-a)^{\frac{1}{p'}}  .
\end{aligned}
\]
Taking \(L^p(\R^d)\) norms above gives
\[
\begin{aligned}
\norm{u}_{L^p(\R^d)}\leq \norm{\partial _du}_{L^p(\R^d)}\frac{p'}{p+p'}(b-a)^{\frac{p+p'}{p'}} \lesssim  \norm{\nabla u}_{L^p(\R^d \to \R^d)} .
\end{aligned}
\]
This concludes the proof.

title: PDEs 3: Sobolev spaces
text:
11 Convolutions and regularization
The convolution of two functions \(f,g\) can be thought of as “blurring” \(f\) by averaging it against \(g\). In the case where \(g\) is smooth, this blurring has the effect of smoothing out any sharp edges and irregularities in \(f\). This allows us to approximate irregular functions by smooth ones and serves as an important technical tool in our analysis.

Definition 17 (Convolution of functions) Given \(f\) and \(g\) we define the convolution of \(f,g\) to be the function \(f *g\)
\[
\begin{aligned}
f *g := \int_{\R^d} f(y)g(x-y) \d y
\end{aligned}
\tag{18}\]

The definition given by (18) is purposefully vague. We still need to specify what spaces \(f,g\) belong to so that \(f*g\) is a well-defined element (of a further unspecified space). This can be done as follows.

Proposition 6 (Young’s convolution inequality) Consider the definition in (18) and let \(p,q,r \in  [1, \infty]\). Then it holds that

If \(f \in L^1(\R^d)\) and \(g \in L^p(\R^d)\) then \(f*g \in L^p(\R^d)\) with
\[
\begin{aligned}
\norm{f*g}_{L^p(\R^d)}\leq \norm{f}_{L^1(\R^d)}\norm{g}_{L^p(\R^d)}.
\end{aligned}
\]
Suppose that
\[
\begin{aligned}
\frac{1}{p}+\frac{1}{q}=1+\frac{1}{r}; \quad f \in L^p(\R^d); \quad g \in L^q(\R^d)    .
\end{aligned}
\]
Then \(f*g \in L^r(\R^d)\) with
\[
\begin{aligned}
\norm{f*g}_{L^r(\R^d)}\leq \norm{f}_{L^p(\R^d)}\norm{g}_{L^q(\R^d)}.
\end{aligned}
\]
In any of the above cases \(f*g=g*f\).



Proof. The first point follows from the triangle inequality for the Bochner integral (in this context this is also called Minkowski’s integral inequality) as
\[
\begin{aligned}
\norm{\int_{\R^d}f(y)g(\cdot -y) \d y}_{L^p(\R^d)}\leq\int_{\R^d}\norm{f(y)g(\cdot -y) \d y}_{L^p(\R^d)}= \norm{f}_{L^1(\R^d)} \norm{g}_{L^p(\R^d)}.
\end{aligned}
\]
To see the second point fix \(f\) and define the linear operator \(T_fg:= f*g\). Then, for \(g \in  L^1(\R^d)\) and \(g \in  L^{p'}(\R^d)\) respectively
\[
\begin{aligned}
\norm{T_f g}_{L^p(\R^d)} \leq \norm{f}_{L^p(\R^d)} \norm{g}_{L^1(\R^d)}; \quad    \norm{T_f g}_{L^\infty(\R^d)} \leq \norm{f}_{L^p(\R^d)} \norm{g}_{L^{p'}(\R^d)};  .
\end{aligned}
\]
Where the first inequality is point one and the second follows from Cauchy-Schwarz. Now applying < https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Riesz

The definition of convolution can be extended to even more settings, for example, suppose that \(g\) is the density of some finite (possibly signed) measure \(\mu\) and \(f\) is bounded, then
\[
\begin{aligned}
f*\mu(x):= f*g(x)= \int_{\R^d}f(x-y) g(y)\d y = \int_{\R^d} f(x-y)\d \mu (y) .
\end{aligned}
\]

Definition 18 (Convolution of function with measure) Let \(\mu\) be a finite signed Borel measure on \(\R^d\) and \(f \in L^p(\R^d)\) then we define the convolution
\[
\begin{aligned}
f*\mu (x) :=\int_{\R^d} f(x-y)\d \mu (y) \in L^p(\R^d).
\end{aligned}
\]

Note that, once more by the triangle inequality, the convolution is well-defined with
\[
\begin{aligned}
\norm{f* \mu }_{L^p(\R^d)} \leq \norm{f}_{L^p(\R^d)} \norm{\mu }_{TV} .
\end{aligned}
\]
Now, if we consider \(f,g\) to be the densities of some finite (signed) measures \(\mu,\nu\) then we obtain that for bounded \(h\)
\[
\begin{aligned}
(h, \mu *\nu) & := \int_{\R^d} h(x) f*g(x) \d x = \int_{\R^d}\int_{\R^d}  h(x+y) f(x)  g(y)\d x \d y \\
& = \int_{\R^d \times\R^d} f(x+y) \d (\mu \otimes \nu)(x,y)\notag .
\end{aligned}
\tag{19}\]
That is, the convolution of \(\mu\) with \(\nu\) is the pushforward of the product measure \(\mu \otimes \nu\) with the sum \(S(x,y)\).

Definition 19 (Convolution of measures) Let \(\mu ,\nu\) be two finite signed measures on \(\Bb(\R^d)\). Then the convolution of \(\mu *\nu\) is the pushforward
\[
\begin{aligned}
\mu *\nu := S\# (\mu \otimes\nu).
\end{aligned}
\]

The language of random variables can give some good motivation for this

Example 3 Let \(X,Y\) be random variables with law \(\mu ,\nu\) then \(X+Y\) has law \(\mu *\nu\). Furthermore, if \(X, Y\) are independent and \(\mu,\nu\) are absolutely continuous with densities \(f,g\) then \(X+Y\) is absolutely continuous with density \(f*g\).


Proof. The first part is by definition of pushforward. To show that \(\mu *\nu\) has density \(f*g\) it suffices to read the reasoning in equation (19) backward.

Through the random variable interpretation, we also see that if \(\mu,\nu\) have all their mass in \(A, B\) then their convolution must have all its mass in \(A+B\). That is,

Lemma 3 Let \(f,g,\mu,\nu\) be such that the convolution is well-defined. Then
\[
\begin{aligned}
\supp{f*g}\subset \overline{\supp{f}+\supp{g}}; \quad \supp{\mu*\nu }=\overline{\supp{\mu }+\supp{\nu}}.
\end{aligned}
\]


Proof. This follows directly from the definition of convolution.

One technical point is that to define the convolution of two objects it is required that they be defined globally. For example if \(U \subsetneq \R^d\), we can’t convolve \(f \in L^p(\R^d)\) with \(g \in L_1(U)\) as the integral
\[
\begin{aligned}
\int_{\R^d}f(y)g(x-y) \d y
\end{aligned}
\]
requires we evaluate \(f\) on all of \(\R^d\). One workaround is, if \(\phi\in L^1(\R^d)\) with \(\supp{\phi}\subset  \overline{B(0,\eps ) }\) we can extend \(f\) to be equal to some \(g \in L^p(\R)\) outside of \(U\)
\[
\begin{aligned}
\tilde{f}(x)=\begin{cases}
f(x) \quad & x \in U     \\
g(x) \quad & x \not\in U
\end{cases}.
\end{aligned}
\]
Then, the convolution \(\tilde{f}*g\) is well defined and equal to
\[
\begin{aligned}
\tilde{f}*\phi(x) = \int_{B(x,\eps )\cap U} f(y)\phi(x-y) \d y+\int_{B(x,\eps )\cap U^c} g(y)\phi(x-y) \d y .
\end{aligned}
\]
As we can see, the convolution in general depends on how we extend \(f\) outside of \(U\). However, it is independent of the extension for \(x\) in
\[
\begin{aligned}
U_\eps :=\set{x \in  U : d(x,\partial U)> \eps }.
\end{aligned}
\]
With
\[
\begin{aligned}
\tilde{f}*\phi(x) = \int_{B(x,\eps )} f(y)\phi(x-y) \d y , \quad\forall x \in  U_\eps   .
\end{aligned}
\]
For this reason, we will employ the following notation.

Definition 20 Given \(f \in L^p(U)\) and \(\phi\in L^1(\R^d)\) with \(\supp{\phi}\subset  \overline{B(0,\eps ) }\) we define \(f*\phi \in L^p(U_\eps )\) as
\[
\begin{aligned}
f*\phi(x):= \int_{B(x,\eps )}f(y)\phi(x-y) \d y .
\end{aligned}
\]

Convolution of distributions with test functions can also be considered. A similar reason to previously leads us to the following definition

Definition 21 Let \(T \in \Dd'(\R^d)\) and \(\varphi \in C_c^\infty(\R^d)\). Then we define the convolution \(T*\varphi \in \Dd^*(\R^d)\) by
\[
\begin{aligned}
T*\varphi(\phi):=T(\tl{\varphi}*\phi) \quad \text{ where } \tl{\varphi}(x):=\varphi(-x) .
\end{aligned}
\]

In the above we can also swap all occurrences of \(C_c^\infty(\R^d)\) and \(\Dd'(\R^d)\) by \(\Ss(\R^d)\) and \(\Ss'(\R^d)\) respectively. An interesting fact is that the convolution of a distribution with a function is itself a function.

Proposition 7 Convolution of distribution and test function is smooth:

Let \(\varphi \in \Dd(\R^d), w \in \Dd'(\R^d)\) then \(\omega * \varphi \in  C^\infty _{\mathrm{loc}}(\R^d)\).
Let \(\varphi \in \Ss(\R^d), w \in \Ss'(\R^d)\) then \(\omega * \varphi \in  C^\infty _{\mathrm{loc}}(\R^d)\).


The previous definitions all go through word by word in the case where we substitute the domain from Euclidean space \(\R^d\) to a LCA group with the Haar measure \(\mu\). For example
\[
\begin{aligned}
f*g(x):= \int_{G}f(y)g(x-y) \d \mu (y).
\end{aligned}
\]
A typical case is when \(G=\T^d\) with the Lebesgue measure or \(G=\Z^d\) with the counting measure. These respectively give
\[
\begin{aligned}
f*g(x)=\int_{\T^d}f(y)g(x-y) \d y; \quad      f*g(k):= \sum_{j\in \Z^d} f(j)g(k-j)  .
\end{aligned}
\]
The same results are also obtained. In fact, save the commutation \(f*g=g*f\), the above results hold even if \(G\) is not Abelian. In this case, one considers the <https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Haar_measure#:~:text=

title: PDEs 3: Sobolev spaces
text:
12 Smoothing in \(L^p\)
In this section, we examine how convolution can be used to approximate functions by smoother ones. This is of great practical use as it allows us to

Consider an appropriate space of functions for our problem, smooth or otherwise.
Perform formal manipulations using the standard rules of calculus as if all functions in this space were smooth and compactly supported until we obtain a desired result.
Pass to the limit to recover the expression for the whole class of functions.

A crucial tool in this program is the following:

Definition 22 We say that a family of functions \(\{\phi_n\}_{n= 1}^\infty \subset L^1(\R^d)\) is an approximation to unity if

Norm 1: \(\norm{\phi_n}_{L^1(\R^d)}=1\).
Decreasing support: \(\supp{\phi_n} \subset B(0, 1 /n)\).

If \(\phi_n \in C_c^\infty(\R^d)\) we say that \(\phi_n\) are smooth.


Remark 5. The above definition is frequently also given letting the index set range over \(\eps \in \R_+\) and taking \(\supp{\phi_\eps } \subset B(0,\eps )\). This is equivalent and simply leads to taking \(\eps \to 0\) instead of \(n \to \infty\).

The first question is whether a smooth approximation to the identity exists. In the following example, we answer this in the affirmative.

Example 4 Let \(\varphi \in C_c^\infty(\R^d)\) then
\[
\begin{aligned}
\phi_n:= \frac{n^d }{\norm{\varphi}_{L^1(\R^d)}}\varphi(nx)
\end{aligned}
\]
is a smooth approximation of the identity. Additionally,
\[
\begin{aligned}
\varphi(x):=  \exp({\frac{1}{\abs{x}^2-1}}) 1_{B(0,1)} \in C_c^\infty(\R^d)
\end{aligned}
\tag{20}\]

By Proposition 6 \((L^1(\R^d),*)\) is a Banach algebra. However, it is a non-unital one. That is there does not exist an element \(e\) such that
\[
\begin{aligned}
f*e=f , \quad\forall f \in L^1(\R^d).
\end{aligned}
\]
However, we will soon see that in a limiting sense, an identity for the convolution exists. First, we need the following lemma.

Lemma 4 The space \(C_c(\R^d)\) is dense in \(L^p(\R^d)\) for all \(p \in [1,\infty)\).


Proof. Consider \(f \in  L^p(\R^d)\). If \(f =1_A\) for some measurable set \(A\) with finite measure then, by the outer and inner regularity of the Lebesgue measure we may take \(U, K\) open and compact respectively with \(U \subset A \subset K\) and
\[
\begin{aligned}
\lambda  (K)-\lambda  (A) <\eps .
\end{aligned}
\]
Where we wrote \(\mu\) for the Lebesgue measure on \(\R^d\). By <https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Urysohn
\[
\begin{aligned}
\norm{f- \varphi}_{L^p(\R^d)} \leq \eps .
\end{aligned}
\]
Since the space of simple functions is dense in \(L^p(\R^d)\) this concludes the proof.

The name “approximation of unity” in Definition Definition 22 is justified by the following proposition.

Proposition 8 Let \(f \in L^p(\R^d)\) where \(p \in [1,\infty)\) and consider \(g \in C_c(\R^d)\) and an approximation to unity \(\phi_n\). Then it holds that
\[
\begin{aligned}
\lim_{n \to \infty}g*\phi_n=g \in  C_c(\R^d);\quad \lim_{n \to \infty}f*\phi_n=f \in  L^p(\R^d).
\end{aligned}
\]


Proof. Consider \(\eps >0\). Using that \(\phi_n\) has mass \(1\) and is supported on \(B(0,1/n)\).
\[
\begin{aligned}
g*\phi_n(x)-g(x) & = \int_{B(0, \frac{1}{n})}(g(x-y)-g(x)) \phi_n(y) \d y
.
\end{aligned}
\]
Now taking norms and \(n\) large enough gives
\[
\begin{aligned}
\norm{g*\phi_n-g}_{L^\infty(\R^d)} \leq \int_{B(0, \frac{1}{n})} \norm{g(\cdot -y) -g}_{L^\infty(\R^d)} \phi_n(y) \d y\leq \eps   .
\end{aligned}
\tag{21}\]
Where in the last inequality we used that \(g\) is uniformly continuous and \(\phi_n\) has mass \(1\). Since \(\eps >0\) was any, this shows the first part of the proposition.
We now prove the second part. By Lemma 4 we can choose \(g\) such that
\[
\begin{aligned}
\norm{g-f}_{L^p(\R^d)}<\eps .
\end{aligned}
\]
Now, since \(K_n:=\supp{g* \phi_n} \subset K + B(0, 1 /n)\), whose measure is bounded by some \(M>0\), the inequality in (21) shows that
\[
\begin{aligned}
\norm{g*\phi_n-g}_{L^p(\R^d)}^p \leq \int_{K_n} \eps^p   \d y\leq M\eps^p  .
\end{aligned}
\]
Now using the triangle inequality and Young’s convolution inequality Proposition 6 gives
\[
\begin{aligned}
\norm{f*\phi_n-f}_{L^p(\R^d)} & \leq\norm{(f-g)*\phi_n}_{L^p(\R^d)}+\norm{g*\phi_n-g}_{L^p(\R^d)}          \\
& +\norm{g-f}_{L^p(\R^d)}\leq \eps + M^{\frac{1}{p}}\eps +\eps .
\end{aligned}
\]
This shows the second part and concludes the proof.

The question is why would we want to approximate a function by its convolutions with some smooth functions the answer is given in the following two results.

Proposition 9 (Smoothing effect) Let \(f \in L^1_{\text{loc}}(\R^d)\) and \(\phi \in C_c^\infty(\R^d)\). Then \(f*\phi \in C^\infty(\R^d)\) with
\[
\begin{aligned}
D^\alpha(f *\phi)=f*D^\alpha \phi , \quad\forall \alpha \in \N^d.
\end{aligned}
\]
Furthermore, if \(f\) is compactly supported then \(f*\phi \in  C_c^\infty(\R^d)\).


Proof. By induction, it suffices to consider the case \(D^\alpha = \partial _i\) for some \(1 \leq i \leq d\). This case can be proved by a differentiation under the integral sign as, given \(\abs{x}\leq M\)
\[
\begin{aligned}
\partial _{x_i} f(y)\phi(x-y) \leq f(y)\norm{\phi}_{C^\infty(K)} 1_{K+B(0,M)}(y) \in L^1(\R^d)
\end{aligned}
\]
Where \(K\) is the support of \(\varphi\).


Remark 6 (Local smoothing). The smoothing effect also holds when \(f\) is only defined on some open set \(U\). Then, with the notation of Definition Definition 20, \(f*\phi \in C^\infty(U_\eps )\) with an identical proof showing
\[
\begin{aligned}
D^\alpha(f *\phi)=f*D^\alpha \phi \text{ on } U_\eps .
\end{aligned}
\]


Theorem 15 It holds that
\[
\begin{aligned}
\overline{C_c^\infty(\R^d)}= L^p(\R^d); \quad \overline{C_c^\infty(\R^d)} = C_c(\R^d).
\end{aligned}
\]
Where the closures are respectively in the \(L^p(\R^d)\) and the uniform topology \((\)given by \(\norm{\cdot }_{\infty})\).


Proof. This follows immediately from Proposition 8 and Proposition 9.


Exercise 29 Let \(U\) be an open subset of \(\R^d\), show that
\[
\begin{aligned}
\overline{C_c^\infty(U)}= L^p(U); \quad \overline{C_c^\infty(U)} = C_c(U).
\end{aligned}
\]







TipHint





Let \(f\) be the non-smooth function to approximate, multiply it first by a mollifier \(\eta_n \in C_c^\infty(U)\) equal to \(1\) in \(V_n\subset U\) (see Example 5). Convolve to get
\[
\begin{aligned}
\varphi_n:=(f \eta_n )*\rho _n.
\end{aligned}
\]
Show as in Theorem 15 that \(\varphi_n\) converges appropriately.



The above can be generalized to non-Euclidean spaces

Theorem 16 Let \((X,\mu )\) be a measure space such that \(X\) is locally convex and Hausdorff and \(\mu\) is inner and outer regular. Then
\[
\begin{aligned}
\overline{C_c(X)} =L^p(X).
\end{aligned}
\]
Suppose additionally that \(X\) is a group, that \(\mu\) is the left or right Haar measure, and that there exists an approximation to unity \(\phi_n\) on \(X\). Then
\[
\begin{aligned}
\overline{C_c^\infty(X)} =L^p(X);\quad \overline{C_c^\infty(X)} =C_c(X).
\end{aligned}
\]


Proof. The first part can be proved by the same method as in Lemma 4 (note that Uryshon’s lemma still holds for LCH spaces). The second part follows by copying the proof of Propositions Proposition 8 and Proposition 9.

The assumption of the existence of an approximation of unity is perhaps the most delicate, but it can be applied for example in the following case.

Corollary 3 It holds that
\[
\begin{aligned}
\overline{C^\infty(\T^d)}= L^p(\T^d); \quad \overline{C^\infty(\T^d)} = C(\T^d).
\end{aligned}
\]

Note that the second part of Corollary 3 also follows from the <https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Stone Similar results hold in the space of distributions.

Proposition 10 Let \(T\in \Dd'(\R^d)\) and \(\varphi_n\) be an approximation to unity. Then
\[
\begin{aligned}
\lim_{n \to \infty} T* \phi_n= T \in \Dd'(\R^d).
\end{aligned}
\]
As a result, \(\overline{C_c^\infty(\R^d)}=\Dd'(\R^d)\).

The proof can be found in (Leoni 2017) page 308.

title: PDEs 3: Sobolev spaces
section: 13 Global to local and back again
text:
13 Global to local and back again
Often it is advantageous to work locally and then reason in the general case by some kind of approximation. A useful tool in this respect are bump functions.

Definition 23 A bump function (also called cutoff function) is a function \(\eta \in C_c^\infty(\R^n)\).

Constructing bump functions that have some desired support is a tool we use frequently throughout. Here we provide two examples to show how this may be done. Other constructions are of course possible.

Example 5 Given two open sets \(V,U\) with \(V \Subset U\) there exists \(\eta \in C_c^\infty(\R^d)\) with support in \(U\), equal to \(1\) on \(V\) and with \(0\leq \eta \leq 1\).


Proof. Since \(V \Subset U\), there exists a compact \(K\) with
\[
\begin{aligned}
V \subset K \subset U;\quad d_u:=d(U,K)>0; \quad d_V:=d(V,K)>0.
\end{aligned}
\]
Now, by <https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Urysohn


Example 6 There exists a sequence of \(\eta_n \in C_c^\infty(\R^d)\) such that
\[
\begin{aligned}
\eta_n(x) =\begin{cases}
1 \text{ if } & \abs{x}\leq n  \\
0 \text{ if } & \abs{x}\geq 2n \\
\end{cases}.
\end{aligned}
\]
And \(\norm{\eta_n}_{C^k(\R^d)} \leq \norm{\eta_1}\).


Proof. Let \(\eta_1 \in C_c^\infty(\R^d)\) be any (for example that of (20)). Then we can take \(\eta_n(x):=\eta(x /n)\).

In the opposite direction. One is often in the situation where it is possible to derive some local properties for a given object (think manifolds). To recover a global result one needs some way to piece together the local results. A useful tool in this respect is partitions of unity.

Definition 24 Given a manifold \(M\) and an open covering \(\{U_\alpha\}_{\alpha \in  J}\) of \(M\) we say that \(\{\rho_i\}_{i\in  I}\) is a partition of unity on \(\{U_\alpha\}_{\alpha \in  J}\) if:

\(\supp{\rho_i}\subset U_\alpha\) for some \(\alpha \in J\).
For each \(x \in M\), it holds that \(x \in \supp{\rho_\alpha}\) for only a finite amount of \(\alpha \in I\).
\(\sum_{i \in I}\rho _i =1.\)


Partitions of unity are often used in differential geometry as follows

Work in some open subset \(\R^n\) (or the upper half space \(\R^n_+\) if our manifold has boundary) to prove the existence of some object \(g\) with desired properties.
Cover the manifold \(M\) with coordinate charts \(U_\alpha\) and translate the euclidean result via the identification with \(U_\alpha\) to obtain locally defined \(g_\alpha\).
Obtain a globally defined object \(g\) by using the partition of unity to piece together the local objects
\[
\begin{aligned}
g=\sum_{\alpha \in I}\rho_\alpha g_\alpha .
\end{aligned}
\]

In addition to the approximation and extension theorems in Section Section 8, partitions of unity can be used to show that: every manifold has a Riemannian metric, show that a function is smooth on some non-open set \(S \subset M\) iff it is the restriction of a smooth function defined on a neighborhood of \(S\), prove the existence of an outward pointing vector on manifolds with boundary, define integration over an orientable manifold \(M\), prove Stokes’ theorem.

Theorem 17 Let \(M\) be a smooth manifold (in particular we assume \(M\) is Hausdorff), then every open covering \(\set{U_\alpha}_{\alpha \in  J}\) has a partition of unity \(\set{\rho _n}_{n=1}^\infty\).

The proof is based on the existence of bump functions (something we have already proved for \(\R^d\)) and is straightforward in the case that \(M\) is compact. The general case can be reduced to the compact setting by obtaining a covering by relatively compact open sets \(\set{U_i}_{i=0}^\infty\) of \(M\) such that \[
\begin{aligned}
V_{i} \Subset V_{i+1}.
\end{aligned}
\]
And then working with the compact \(\overline{V_{i+1}} \setminus V_{i}\). See (Tu 2011) Appendix C for the details.

title: PDEs 3: Sobolev spaces
text:
14 Manifolds with boundary
We will be defining differential equations on open domains \(\Omega \subset \R^n\). In this case \(\overline{\Omega}\) will be a “manifold with boundary” whose regularity will determine what results we have access to. The prototypical example of a manifold with a boundary is the upper half space
\[
\begin{aligned}
\Hh^d:=\set{x=(x_1,\ldots,x_d)\in \R^d: x_d \geq 0} .
\end{aligned}
\]
Here the inequality is not strict so that the boundary of \(\Hh^d\) is included in itself. Since \(\Hh^d\) is not open we need to define what is meant by saying that a function is differentiable on such a set.

Definition 25 Let \(S \subset \R^d\) be an arbitrary set. We say that a function \(f:S \to \R\) is \(k\)-times differentiable at \(p\) if there exists a function \(\tl{f}:\R^d \to \R\) which is \(k\) times differentiable at \(p\) and such that \(\tl{f}=f\) on \(S\).


Definition 26 If \(f\) is \(k\)-times differentiable at every point of \(S\) we say that \(f \in C^k(S)\).


Exercise 30 Show that \(f\in  C^k(S)\) if and only if there exists an extension \(\tl{f}\in C^k(\R^d)\) of \(f\) which is equal to \(f\) on \(S\).







TipHint





Use a partition of unity.



A manifold with boundary \(M\) is just a generalization of \(\Hh^d\), where we impose that \(M\) is “locally equal” to \(\Hh^d\). That is, there exists a covering of \(M\) by open sets \(V_\alpha\) and a collection of homeomorphisms with the subspace topology from \(\Hh^d\)
\[
\begin{aligned}
\Phi_\alpha : V_\alpha \iso  \Phi(V_\alpha).
\end{aligned}
\tag{22}\]
And where for compatibility we impose that for each \(\alpha,\beta\) the functions
\[
\begin{aligned}
\Phi_\beta \circ\Phi_\alpha^{-1}: \Phi_\alpha(V_\alpha\cap V_\beta ) \longmapsto \Phi_\beta (V_\alpha\cap V_\beta )
\end{aligned}
\tag{23}\]
are diffeomorphisms on \(\Hh^d\) (again with the subspace topology). Here \(\Aa=\set{(V_\alpha,\Phi_\alpha)}\) is an atlas of \(M\). We say that \(\Aa\) is \(C^k\) if the diffeomorphisms in (23) are \(C^k\) (see Definition Definition 25).

Definition 27 We say that \((M,\Aa)\) is a \(C^k\) manifold with boundary if \(M\) is a second countable Hausdorff space and \(\Aa\) is a \(C^k\) atlas.

The boundary of \(M\) is its points that are mapped to the boundary \(\set{x_d=0}\) in \(\Hh^d\).

Definition 28 Given a point \(p \in M\) we say that \(p\) is a boundary point if for some (and thus every chart) \(\Phi_\alpha(p) \in \partial \Hh ^{d}\). We call the set of all boundary points the boundary of \(M\) and denote it by \(\partial M\).


Exercise 31 Show that the restricted atlas \(\restr{\Phi_\alpha}{\partial M}\) makes \(\partial M\) a \(d-1\) dimensional manifold without boundary.







TipHint





By definition of boundary
\[
\begin{aligned}
\restr{\Phi_\alpha}{\partial M}: V_\alpha \cap M \to \partial \Hh^{d-1} \simeq \R^{d-1}.
\end{aligned}
\tag{24}\]
And the coordinate changes are \(C^k\) as the restriction of a \(C^k\) map is \(C^k\).



In our case we will always take \(M\) to be a subset of \(\R^d\), in this case, different variations of the above definition are possible. For example, by the inverse function theorem and Exercise 30, we can extend the functions \(\Phi_\alpha\) to diffeomorphisms on \(U_\alpha\) open in \(\R^d\) so that (22)-(24) now read
\[
\begin{aligned}
\Phi_\alpha : U_\alpha \iso  \Phi(U_\alpha); \quad    \Phi_\alpha : U_\alpha \cap M \iso  \Phi_\alpha(U_\alpha) \cap \Hh^d .
\end{aligned}
\tag{25}\]
Additionally, by the implicit function theorem there exists for each coordinate set \(U_\alpha\) functions \(\gamma_\alpha \in C^k(\R^{d-1})\) such that, relabeling the coordinates and decreasing the size of \(U_\alpha\) if necessary,
\[
\begin{aligned}
\partial M \cap U_\alpha & =\set{x \in U_\alpha : x_d=\gamma_\alpha(x_1,\ldots,x_{d-1}) }
\end{aligned}
\tag{26}\]
Let us write \(x=(x',x_d)\), by the Taylor expansion
\[
\begin{aligned}
\Phi_\alpha(x',\gamma _\alpha(x')+\eps)=\Phi_\alpha(x)+\frac{\partial \Phi}{\partial x_d}(x)\eps +O(\norm{\eps }^2)  .
\end{aligned}
\]
We deduce that, once more reducing the size of \(U_\alpha\) if necessary and depending on the sign of \(\partial _d \Phi\) on \(U_\alpha\), one and only one of the following two hold
\[
\begin{aligned}
(M \setminus \partial M) \cap U_\alpha & =\set{x \in U_\alpha : x_d>\gamma_\alpha(x_1,\ldots,x_{d-1}) }        \\
(M \setminus \partial M) \cap U_\alpha & =\set{x \in U_\alpha : x_d<\gamma_\alpha(x_1,\ldots,x_{d-1}) }\notag.
\end{aligned}
\tag{27}\]
The equivalent formulations in (25) and in (26)-(27) are used in the main exposition. Finally, in our case, we will typically take \(M =\overline{\Omega}\) where \(\Omega \subset \R^d\) is some open set. In this case, we adopt the following terminology.

Definition 29 We say that an open set \(\Omega\subset \R^d\) has \(C^k\) boundary of \(\overline{\Omega}\) is a \(C^k\) manifold with boundary.

In the above case, the topological and manifold boundaries of \(\Omega\) necessarily coincide as homeomorphisms map topological boundaries to topological boundaries.

title: PDEs 3: Sobolev spaces
text:
15 References


Brezis, Haim, and Haim Brézis. 2011. Functional Analysis, Sobolev Spaces and Partial Differential Equations. Vol. 2. 3. Springer. https://link.springer.com/book/10.1007/978-0-387-70914-7.


Evans, Lawrence C. 2022. Partial Differential Equations. Vol. 19. American Mathematical Society. https://math24.files.wordpress.com/2013/02/partial-differential-equations-by-evans.pdf.


Kinnunen, Juha. 2023. Sobolev Spaces. https://math.aalto.fi/~jkkinnun/files/sobolev_spaces.pdf.


Leoni, Giovanni. 2017. A First Course in Sobolev Spaces. American Mathematical Soc. https://bookstore.ams.org/gsm-105#:~:text=A%20First%20Course%20in%20Sobolev%20Spaces&text=Sobolev%20spaces%20are%20a%20fundamental,BV%20functions%20of%20one%20variable.


Taylor, Michael. 2013. Partial Differential Equations II: Qualitative Studies of Linear Equations. Vol. 116. Springer Science & Business Media.


Tu, Loring W. 2011. “Manifolds.” In An Introduction to Manifolds. Springer. https://link.springer.com/chapter/10.1007/978-1-4419-7400-6_3.

href: posts/PDEs/1. The Fourier transform, tempered distributions and Sobolev spaces/index.html
title: PDEs 1: The Fourier transform, tempered distributions and Sobolev spaces
text:
The Fourier transform defines a linear isometry on the space of square-integrable complex-valued functions and has as inverse the inverse Fourier transform. This allows us to write these functions as a superposition of harmonic functions.
Linear differential operators act as a simple polynomial multiplication on the frequency domain. Smooth functions have Fourier transforms that decay quickly and vice-versa. This correspondence also allows us to introduce fractional differential operators and pseudo differential operators such as \(\sqrt{-\Delta }\).
The Sobolev spaces \(H^s\) and tempered distributions \(\Ss'\) are complete spaces of functions to which we can extend differential operators.

This correspondence also allows us to introduce fractional differential operators and pseudo differential operators such as \(\sqrt{-\Delta }\).

title: PDEs 1: The Fourier transform, tempered distributions and Sobolev spaces
text:
Introduction
Hi everyone, welcome to the first post in a new series focusing on partial differential equations (PDEs). In this post, we’ll introduce some of the key analytical tools used to solve them. These tools will play a prominent role in the future posts of this series and in other series to come. We’ll begin by introducing some notation and necessary preliminaries on integration. Next, we will introduce the Fourier transform, which is the fundamental object of harmonic analysis and plays a fundamental role in partial differential equations (PDEs), probability theory, and number theory. In particular, we will show in future posts how many linear PDEs such as the heat equation can be solved using it.
After this, we’ll discuss the generalization of what it means to solve a PDE. To do so we will need to introduce the space of (tempered) distributions \(\Ss'\) and Sobolev spaces \(H^s\). These two spaces will be crucial in allowing us to extend the concept of solution to PDE to these spaces and use their completeness to prove the existence, uniqueness, and regularity of solutions.
Much of the material here contained can be found in my <https://gredos.usal.es/bitstream/handle/10366/145629/TFG

Notation
We write \(L^p(\R^d)\) and \(L^p(\R^d\to\C^m)\) to denote the space of equivalence classes of \(p\) integrable functions from \(\R^d\) to \(\R\) and \(\C^m\) respectively. Given a function defined on a factor space \(u:X\times Y\rightarrow Z\) we shall use \(u(x)\) to stand for the slightly more cumbersome \(u(x,\cdot ):Y\rightarrow Z\). Given \(\alpha=(\alpha_1,...,\alpha_d)\in\N^d\) we shall write as is standard
\[x^\alpha:=x^{\alpha_1}\cdots  x^{\alpha_d};\quad D^\alpha:=\partial_1^{\alpha_1}\cdots\partial_1^{\alpha_d}\]
and whenever the expression \(D^\alpha\) appears we will assume implicitly that \(\alpha\in\N^d\). Given \(x\in\R^d\) we will write \(\abs{x}\) to denote its norm and the Japanese bracket \(\br{x}\) will signify
\[\br{x}:=(1+\abs{x}^2)^{1/2}.\]
Finally, we will employ the notation \(f\lesssim g\) to mean that there exists some constant \(C\) such that \(f\leq Cg\). If the value of \(C\) depends on some other parameter such as the dimension \(d\) we shall make this explicit by writing \(f\lesssim_d g\).


Some useful results on integration
In PDEs, it is essential to be able to commute limits and differentials with both summation and integration. The main way of doing this is the dominated and monotone convergence theorem together with the following two propositions which we state in full generality for functions valued in a Banach space \(E\). Though for now, we will only need the case \(E=\R\). On first reading, this can be assumed for commodity.

Proposition 1 (Continuity of the integral) Let \((X,\mu )\) be a measure space, \(T\) a first countable metric space, \((E,\norm{})\) a Banach space and
\[f:T\times X\to E\]
such that \(f(t)\) is (Bochner) integrable for all \({t}\in{T}\) and such that
\[\norm{f(t)}\leq g\in L^1(X)\quad \forall{t}\in {T}.\]
Then given \(t_0\in T\) we have that
\[\lim_{t\to t_0}\int_X f(t,x)\mu(dx) =\int_X\lim_{t\to t_0}f(t,x)\mu(dx) .\]
In consequence, if \(f(x)\) is continuous so is \(\int_X f(t,x) \mu(dx)\).


Proof. The proof is an application of the dominated convergence theorem to the sequence of functions \(f_n:=f(t_n)\) where \(\{t_n\}_{n=1}^\infty\) is some sequence converging to \(t\) and the fact that, in first countable spaces, sequential continuity is equivalent to continuity.


Proposition 2 ({Differentiation under the integral sign}) Let \(X\) be a measure space, \(U\) be a convex open interval of \(\R\), and \(E\) a Banach space. Consider
\[f:U\times X\to E\]
such that:

\(f(t)\) is measurable for every \(t\in U\) and integrable for some \(t_0\in U\).
For each \(x\in X\) we have that \(f(x)\) is differentiable and there exists an integrable function \(g: X\to\R\) such that
\[\norm{\partial_{t}{f}(t,x)}\leq g(x)\quad \forall{(t,x)}\in {U\times E}.\]
Then it holds that
\[\partial_{t}{\int_X f(t)\mu(dx)}=\int_X \partial_{t}f(t) \mu(dx).\]



Proof. First, we show that the integral on the left-hand side of the equation above makes sense. Let \(t\in U\) be any, then by the mean value inequality we have that
\[
\frac{f(t)-f(t_0)}{t-t_0}\leq \sup_{s \in U}\norm{\partial _t f(s)}\leq g \in L^1(X).
\]
Thus, we may apply the previous proposition to commute the limit with the integral
\[
\begin{aligned}
\lim_{t \to t_0}\frac{\int_{X} f(t)\mu (dx)-\int_{X}f(t_0) \mu (dx)}{t-t_0}  &=\lim_{t \to t_0}\int_{X}    \frac{f(t)-f(t_0)}{t-t_0} \mu (dx)
\\&=\int_{X}\lim_{t \to t_0} \frac{f(t)-f(t_0)}{t-t_0} \mu (dx) =\int_{X}\partial _t f(t) \mu(dx).
\end{aligned}
\]
Where in the first equality we also used the linearity of the Bochner integral and in the last equality we used that by hypothesis \(f(x)\) is everywhere differentiable. This concludes the proof.

In the literature the usual hypothesis is that \(\partial _t f\) is integrable everywhere (Aguirre and Ibáñez 2002, 108), however as we have seen it is sufficient to require the integrability at a point.

objectID: posts/PDEs/1. The Fourier transform, tempered distributions and Sobolev spaces/index.html#the-fourier-transform-on-rd
href: posts/PDEs/1. The Fourier transform, tempered distributions and Sobolev spaces/index.html#the-fourier-transform-on-rd
title: PDEs 1: The Fourier transform, tempered distributions and Sobolev spaces
text:
1 The Fourier transform on \(\R^d\)
These preliminaries out of the way, we now get to our first main section. We assume the reader has encountered the Fourier transform before, and limit ourselves to giving a rundown of the theory and main ideas, taking the reader through a basic definition to an extension of it. To make the Fourier transform an isometry we will use the following convention.

Definition 1 Given \(f\in L^1(\R^d\to\C^m)\) the Fourier transform of \(f\) and the inverse Fourier transform of \(f\) are defined respectively by
\[\hat{f}(\xi):=\int_{\R^d}f(x)e^{-2\pi i\xi\cdot  x} dx, \quad \check{f}(\xi):=\int_{\R^d}f(x)e^{2\pi i\xi\cdot  x} dx \]

Other exponents such as \(e^{-ix}, e^{-\pi i x}\) can also be used with identical results modulo constants. Let us introduce the notation \[\Ff_1 f:=\hat{f}, \Ff_1^*f\] for the Fourier transform (on \(L^1\)) and its inverse. The following proposition holds.

Proposition 3 (Properties of \(\Ff_1\)) Given \(f\in L^1(\R^d\to\C^m)\)

The Fourier transform \(\Ff_1\) is a continuous linear operators \[\Ff_1:L^1(\R^d\to\C^m) \to L^\infty(\R^d\to\C^m)\].
If \(x^\alpha f(x)\in L^1(\R^d\to\C^m)\) then:
\[D^\alpha \hat{f}(\xi)=(-2\pi i)^\abs{\alpha}\widehat{x^{\alpha} f}(\xi)\qquad\forall \xi\in\R^d.\]
If \(f\) is absolutely continuous in \(x_j\) for almost every \(x_1,...x_{j-1},x_{j+1},...,x_d\) then then
\[\widehat{\partial x_j f}(\xi)=2\pi i\xi_j\widehat{ f}(\xi)\qquad\forall \xi\in\R^d.\]



Proof. The proof of the first point is an application of the triangle inequality as
\[
\abs{\hat{f}(\xi )}=\abs{\int_{\R^d}f(x)e^{2\pi i x\cdot \xi } dx}\leq\int_{\R^d}\abs{f(x)}dx\quad\forall \xi \in \R^d.
\]
and similarly for the inverse. The second point follows by differentiation under the integral Proposition 2. The third point can be proved by using integration by parts and the observation that every absolutely continuous function must go to zero at infinity.

We note that the analogous holds for the inverse Fourier transform where it is only necessary to remove the minus sign in \(2\) and a change of the sign in \(3\). The proof can be completed in an identical fashion or by noting that \(\check{f}(\xi )=\hat{f}(-\xi )\).
The importance of these last two properties is that they give us information on the Fourier transform of a function \(f\) based on its regularity and decay. They can be stated informally as decay gives regularity and regularity gives decay (as the Fourier transform of any \(f\in L^1(\R^d\to\R^d)\) decays to 0 at infinity by the <https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Riemann In general, the Fourier transform of an integrable function is not itself integrable. That is, \(L^1(\R^d\to\C^m)\) is not closed under the Fourier transform. We now introduce a space that is closed under the Fourier transform; the Schwartz space, which can be thought of as the space of infinitely regular functions with infinite decay:

Definition 2 The Schwartz space \(\mathcal{S}(\R^d\to\C^m)\) is:
\[\mathcal{S}(\R^d\to\C^m):=\lbrace f\in\C^\infty(\R^d\to\C^m):x^\alpha D^\beta f\in L^\infty(\R^d\to\C^m)\quad\forall\alpha,\beta\in \N^d\rbrace.\]

By applying Proposition 3 we deduce that, given \(f\in\mathcal{S}(\R^d\to\C^m)\)
\[
\begin{aligned}
\xi^\alpha D^\beta \hat{f}(\xi)   & =(-2\pi i)^{\abs{\beta}}\xi^{\alpha}\widehat{x^{\beta}f}=\frac{(-2\pi i)^{\abs{\beta}}}{(2\pi i)^{\abs{\alpha}}}\widehat{D^{\alpha}x^{\beta}f}\in L^\infty(\R^d\to\C^m)\\
\xi^\alpha D^\beta \check{f}(\xi) & =\frac{(2\pi i)^{\abs{\beta}}}{(-2\pi i)^{\abs{\alpha}}}\widehat{D^{\alpha}x^{\beta}f}\in L^\infty(\R^d\to\C^m).
\end{aligned}
\tag{1}\]
Hence, the Fourier transform and the inverse Fourier transform restrict to endomorphisms of the Schwartz space which we will denote respectively by
\[\mathcal{F}_\mathcal{S},\mathcal{F}^*_\mathcal{S}:\mathcal{S}(\R^d\to\C^m)\to\mathcal{S}(\R^d\to\C^m).\]
The next item on the agenda is Plancherel’s theorem which is proven via the following lemma, in which we shall use the notation
\[\br{f,g}_{L^2(\R^d\to\C^m)}:=\int_{\R^d}f(x)\cdot  \overline{g(x)} dx\]
for the inner product on \(L^2(\R^d\to\C^m)\).

Lemma 1 (Plancherel for Schwartz functions) Given \(f,g\in \mathcal{S}(\R^d\to\C^m)\)

\(\br{\mathcal{F}_\mathcal{S}f,g}_{L^2(\R^d\to\C^m)}=\br{f,\mathcal{F}_\mathcal{S}^*g}_{L^2(\R^d\to\C^m)};\quad\br{\mathcal{F}_\mathcal{S}^*f,g}_{L^2(\R^d\to\C^m)}=\br{f,\mathcal{F}_\mathcal{S}g}_{L^2(\R^d\to\C^m)}\)
\(\mathcal{F}_\mathcal{S}^*\mathcal{F}_\mathcal{S}f=\mathcal{F}_\mathcal{S}\mathcal{F}_\mathcal{S}^*f=f\)


The first point can be proved by a direct application of Fubini. The second point is trickier and is the crux of why we call \(\Ff^*\) the inverse Fourier transform. The proof can be found in (Taylor 1996) pages 222-226. From Lemma 1 and Lemma 1 we deduce immediately that, given a Schwartz function \(f\),
\[
\norm{f}_{L^2(\R^d\to\C^m)}=\norm{\mathcal{F}_\mathcal{S}f}_{L^2(\R^d\to\C^m)}=\norm{\mathcal{F}_\mathcal{S}^*f}_{L^2(\R^d\to\C^m)}
\tag{2}\]
That is, the restrictions of \(\Ff_1\) and \(\Ff^*_1\)
\[\mathcal{F}_\mathcal{S},\mathcal{F}_\mathcal{S}^*:\mathcal{S}(\R^d\to\C^m)\to\mathcal{S}(\R^d\to\C^m)\]
are linear unitary operators which are the inverse the one to the other. We obtain the following result.

Proposition 4 (Plancherel’s theorem) \(\mathcal{F}_\mathcal{S}\) and \(\mathcal{F}_\mathcal{S}^*\) may be extended to unitary operators:
\[\mathcal{F},\mathcal{F}^*:L^2(\R^d\to\C^m)\to L^2(\R^d\to\C^m)\]
With \(\Ff\Ff^*=\Ff^*\Ff=Id\). \end {proposition}

Proof. This is a immediate result of the density of \(\mathcal{S}(\R^d\to\C^m)\) in the larger \(L^2(\R^d\to\C^m)\) and the completeness of \(L^2(\R^d\to\C^m)\) together with Lemma 1 and Plancherel for \(\Ss\) Lemma 1. As, by a simple limiting argument, it is easy to see that continuous extensions of continuous linear operators preserve the norm and the inverses of the operators in question.

It is reasonable to wonder if this extended Fourier transform \(\Ff\) coincides with our initial definition when reasonable. That is, whether given a function \(f \in L^1(\R^d\to\C^m)\cap L^2(\R^d\to\C^m)\) we have that
\[\Ff f(\xi)=\Ff_1f(\xi)=\int_{\R^d}f(x)e^{-2\pi ix\cdot \xi} dx\qquad\forall \xi\in\R^d.\]
This indeed holds as can be seen by taking a sequence of functions \(\lbrace f_n\rbrace_{n=1}^\infty\in\Ss(\R^d\to\C^m)\) converging to \(f\) in \(L^1(\R^d\to\C^m)\) and in \(L^2(\R^d\to\C^m)\). As then \(\Ff_1 f_n\) converges uniformly to \(\Ff_1f\) by Proposition 3 (The) and in \(L^2(\R^d\to\C^m)\) to \(\Ff f\) by Plancherel Proposition 4, from where we deduce that as desired \(\Ff_1f=\Ff f\).

2 The Fourier transform of periodic functions
We now extend our previous results to \(\Z^d\) periodic functions, though, for any period, analogous results may be achieved. By identifying \(\Z^d\) periodic functions with functions on the torus \(\T^d:=\R^d/\Z^d\equiv [0,1]^d\) we will work with functions \(f:\T^d\to\C^m.\) As we will see strikingly similar results are achieved in this setting

Definition 3 Given \(f\in L^1(\T^d\to\C^m)\) we define the \(k\)-th Fourier coefficient of \(f\) as:
\[\hat{f}(k):=\int_{\T^d}f(x)e^{-2\pi ik\cdot x}dx.\]

We thus obtain a function \(\hat{f}\) on \(\Z^d\) which we shall call the Fourier series of \(f\) and a continuous linear function which we shall denote as in the euclidean case:
\[\Ff_1:L^1(\T^d\to\C^m)\to l^\infty(\Z^d\to\C^m)\]
where \(l^{\infty}(\Z^d\to\C^m)\) is the set of bounded sequences from \(\Z^d\) to \(\C^m\). As before we have the following result: \begin{proposition} Given \(f\in L^1(\T^d\to\C^m)\) if \(f\) is absolutely continuous in \(x_j\) for almost every \(x_1,...x_{j-1},x_{j+1},...,x_d\) then
\[\widehat{\partial_{x_j}{f}}(k)=2\pi i k_j\widehat{f}(k)\qquad\forall k\in\Z^d.\]


In particular if \(f\in C^\infty(\T^d\to\C^m)\) we have that:
\[
\widehat{D^\alpha f}(k)=(2\pi ik)^\alpha\hat{f}(k),
\tag{3}\]
and as a result we have that \(\hat{f}\) is of rapid decrease (i.e. \(\hat{f}\) decreases faster than the inverse of any polynomial). We thus have, as before, an induced map:
\[\Ff_{C^\infty}:C^\infty(\T^d\to\C^m)\to s(\Z^d\to\C^m)\]
\[f\mapsto \hat{f}.\]
Where \(s(\Z^d\to\C^m)\) are the sequences from \(\Z^d\) to \(\C^m\) that are of rapid decrease (this space now plays the role of the Schwartz space \(\Ss(\R^d\to\C^m)\)).
Similarly to the non-periodic case we now define
\[\Ff^*_{C^\infty}:s(\Z^d\to\C^m)\to C^\infty(\T^d\to\C^m);\quad a\mapsto\check{a}\]
with:
\[\check{a}(x):=\sum_{k\in\Z^d}a(k)e^{2\pi ik\cdot x}.\]
This is indeed smooth as, by the rapid decay of \(a\), we may differentiate under the integral sign Proposition 2 (we recall that sums are just integrals against discrete measures) to commute all derivatives with the above sum to obtain that
\[
D^\alpha \check{a}(x)=\sum_{k\in\Z^d}(2\pi ik)^\alpha a(k)e^{2\pi ik\cdot x}\quad\forall{a}\in {s(\Z^d\to\C^m)}.
\tag{4}\]
It is now possible to prove as with the euclidean case that
\[\Ff_{C^\infty}\Ff^*_{C^\infty}=\Ff^*_{C^\infty}\Ff_{C^\infty}=Id,\]
and that analogously given \(f\) smooth, and \(a\) of rapid decrease
\[\br{\Ff_{C^\infty} f,a}_{l^2(\Z^d\to\C^m)}=\br{f,\Ff_{C^\infty}^*a}_{L^2(\R^d\to\C^m)};\quad\br{\Ff_{C^\infty}^* a,f}_{L^2(\R^d\to\C^m)}=\br{a,\Ff f}_{l^2(\Z^d\to\C^m)}.\]
See for example (Taylor 1996) pages 197-206. We conclude that \(\Ff_{C^\infty}\) are unitary linear functions and that hence:

Proposition 5 (Plancherel’s (periodic) theorem) \(\mathcal{F}_{C^\infty}\) and \(\mathcal{F}_{C^\infty}^*\) may be extended to unitary operators:
\[\mathcal{F}:L^2(\T^d\to\C^m)\to l^2(\T^d\to\C^m);\quad \mathcal{F}^*:l^2(\Z^d\to\C^m)\to L^2(\R^d\to\C^m)\]
with \(\Ff\Ff^*=\Ff^*\Ff=Id.\)

We note that, as for the Euclidean case, given \(f\in L^2(\T^d\to\C^m)\cap L^1(\T^d\to\C^m)\) by an identical argument it holds that
\[\Ff f(k)=\Ff_1f(k)=\int_{\R^d}f(x)e^{-2\pi ik\cdot x}\]
and where now Plancherel’s theorem gives that, for such \(f\):
\[
f(x)=\sum_{k\in\Z^d}\hat{f}(k)e^{2\pi ik\cdot x}.
\tag{5}\]

2 The Fourier transform of periodic functions
We now extend our previous results to \(\Z^d\) periodic functions, though, for any period, analogous results may be achieved. By identifying \(\Z^d\) periodic functions with functions on the torus \(\T^d:=\R^d/\Z^d\equiv [0,1]^d\) we will work with functions \(f:\T^d\to\C^m.\) As we will see strikingly similar results are achieved in this setting

Definition 3 Given \(f\in L^1(\T^d\to\C^m)\) we define the \(k\)-th Fourier coefficient of \(f\) as:
\[\hat{f}(k):=\int_{\T^d}f(x)e^{-2\pi ik\cdot x}dx.\]

We thus obtain a function \(\hat{f}\) on \(\Z^d\) which we shall call the Fourier series of \(f\) and a continuous linear function which we shall denote as in the euclidean case:
\[\Ff_1:L^1(\T^d\to\C^m)\to l^\infty(\Z^d\to\C^m)\]
where \(l^{\infty}(\Z^d\to\C^m)\) is the set of bounded sequences from \(\Z^d\) to \(\C^m\). As before we have the following result: \begin{proposition} Given \(f\in L^1(\T^d\to\C^m)\) if \(f\) is absolutely continuous in \(x_j\) for almost every \(x_1,...x_{j-1},x_{j+1},...,x_d\) then
\[\widehat{\partial_{x_j}{f}}(k)=2\pi i k_j\widehat{f}(k)\qquad\forall k\in\Z^d.\]

href: posts/PDEs/1. The Fourier transform, tempered distributions and Sobolev spaces/index.html#sec-distrsec
title: PDEs 1: The Fourier transform, tempered distributions and Sobolev spaces
text:
3 Distributions and Sobolev Spaces
Here we will quickly recall the concepts of tempered distributions and Sobolev spaces, which are concepts of utmost importance in the field of PDEs and Fourier analysis. The idea is as follows, given a topological vector space \(V\) we denote the dual of \(V\) by
\[V':=\lbrace w:E\to\C: w \text{continuous}\rbrace.\]
Furthermore, given \(w\in V'\) and \(u\in V\) we use the notation
\[
(u,w):=w(u)
\]
The reason for this is that \(w(u)\) can often be interpreted as the inner product of \(w\) against \(u\) in a suitable Hilbert space. In our case, this Hilbert space will be \(L^2(\R^d\to\C)\) and the above interpretation will allow us to extend differentiation and the Fourier transform by a technique called “duality”. We will apply this where \(V=\Ss(\R^d\to\C)\), first we need to introduce a topology on \(E\). In general, given a real vector space \(V\) together with a countable family of semi-norms \(\lbrace p_j\rbrace_{j=0}^\infty\) with the property that: given \(x\neq 0\), there exists \(j\) such that \(p_j(x)\neq 0\). Then
\[
d(x,y):=\sum_{j=0}^\infty 2^{-j}\frac{p_j(x-y)}{1+p_j(x-y)}\quad \forall{x,y}\in {E}
\tag{6}\]
is a translation invariant metric on \(E\). In the case of the Schwartz space \(\Ss(\R^d\to\C)\) we give it the topology induced by
\[
p_k(u):=\sum_{\abs{\alpha}\leq k} \sup_{x\in\R^d}\br{x}^k \abs{D^\alpha u(x)}.
\tag{7}\]
Though other families of semi-norms the reader may be familiar with such as
\[p_{k,\alpha}:=\sup_{x\in\R^d}\abs{x}^k \abs{D^\alpha u(x)};\quad\text{or}\quad p'_{k,\alpha}:=\sup_{x\in\R^d}(1+\abs{x})^k \abs{D^\alpha u(x)}\]
induce equivalent topologies. We note that with this metric \(\Ss(\R^d\to\C)\) is complete.
For a quick proof based on the fundamental theorem of calculus see for example (Folland 1999) page 237.

Definition 4 The space of tempered distributions is the dual space to \(\Ss(\R^d\to\C)\) with the topology generated by \(p_k)\). We write it \(\Ss'(\R^d\to\C)\).

One may verify that we have the inclusion
\[
\begin{aligned}
L^p(\R^d\to\C)\hookrightarrow\Ss'(\R^d\to\C);\quad f\mapsto T_f
\end{aligned}
\tag{8}\]
where given \(u\in \Ss(\R^d\to\C)\) we define
\[T_f(u):=\br{u,f}:=\int_{\R^d} u\overline{f}.\]
Let us write \(T^t\) for the transpose of a linear function \(T\) and recall that the Fourier transform is an endomorphism of the Schwartz space. Then, given two Schwartz functions \(u,v\) we have already seen that, by a simple application of Fubini,
\[T_{\Ff v}(u)=\br{u,\Ff v}=\br{\Ff^{-1} u,v}=(\Ff^{-1}u,T_v)\]
and integration by parts gives
\[
\begin{aligned}
T_{D^\alpha v}(u)=\br{u,D^\alpha v}=(-1)^{\abs{\alpha}}\br{D^\alpha u,v}=((-1)^{\abs{\alpha}}D^\alpha u,T_v)\quad\forall{\alpha}\in \N^d.
\end{aligned}
\]
This gives us a way of extending the Fourier transform and differentiation to the space of tempered distributions.

Definition 5 Given \(w\in\mathcal{S}'(\R^d\to\C)\) and \(\alpha\in\N^d\) we define the (distributional) Fourier transform of \(w\) by
\[\Ff w:= w\circ \Ff^{-1}\]
and the (weak) \(\alpha\)’th derivative of \(w\) by
\[D^\alpha w:= w\circ((-1)^\abs{\alpha}D^\alpha).\]

The method we used above to extend \(\Ff, D^\alpha\) is called the duality method and appears very frequently. Another way of writing the above definition is:
\[
(u, \Ff w):=(\Ff^{-1} u, w);\quad(u,D^\alpha w):=(-1)^\abs{\alpha}(D^\alpha u,w)
\]
Due to our previous discussion, we have that with this definition
\[
\Ff T_u=T_{\Ff u};\quad D^\alpha T_{u}=T_{D^\alpha u}\quad\forall u\in \Ss(\R^d).
\tag{9}\]
Just as one would desire.
Two other operations that are permitted on \(\Ss(\R^d\to\C)\) are multiplication by functions of polynomial growth \(p\) and the application of the inverse Fourier transform which we shall, as for \(L^2\) functions, denote by \(\Ff^{-1}\). Both definitions are once again being given by duality.
\[
(u,pw):=(\overline{p}u,w);\quad (u,\Ff^{-1}w):=(\Ff u,w) .
\]
Before ending our discussion of (scalar) tempered distributions we comment on some generalizations. We first note that the previous discussion works equivalently for vector-valued distributions, i.e. elements of the dual to \(\mathcal{S}(\R^d\to\C^m)\), which we shall denote by
\[\mathcal{S}'(\R^d\to\C^m)\]
where the only change is that the inclusion of integrable functions is now given by
\[
\begin{aligned}
L^p(\R^d\to\C^m)\hookrightarrow\Ss'(\R^d\to\C^m);\quad f\mapsto T_f
\end{aligned}
\tag{10}\]
with \(T_f\) defined by
\[
\begin{aligned}
T_f(u):=\int_{\R^d} u\cdot \bar{f}\quad\forall{u}\in {\Ss(\R^d\to\C^m)}.
\end{aligned}
\tag{11}\]
In both cases we have that, by duality, due to the (3), given a tempered distribution \(w\) and \(\alpha\in \N^d\)
\[
\begin{aligned}
D^\alpha\Ff w =(-2\pi i)^{\abs{\alpha}}\Ff x^\alpha w;\quad
\Ff D^{\alpha} w= (2\pi i)^{\abs{\alpha}}x^\alpha\Ff w.
\end{aligned}
\tag{12}\]
As we have observed before multiplication by functions of polynomial growth is a well-defined operation on \(\Ss(\R^d\to\C^m)\) so the above expressions are also well defined. A quick verification also shows that, since Plancherel’s theorem holds for all Schwartz functions,
\[
\Ff^{-1}\Ff w=\Ff\Ff^{-1}w=w.
\tag{13}\]
Finally, in addition to “changing the image” of our distributions, we may also “change the domain” by considering, for example, periodic tempered distributions. Where now, as we saw in the section on the Fourier transform, \(C^\infty(\T^d\to\C^m)\) takes the place of the Schwartz space and where we place on \(C^\infty(\T^d\to\C^m)\) the topology defined by the countable family of semi-norms:
\[q_k(u):=\sum_{\abs{\alpha}\leq k}\sup_{x\in\T^d}\abs{D^\alpha u}\]
and denote its dual by \(\Ss'(\T^d\to\C^m).\) Note that, as the domain is bounded, multiplication by polynomials to ensure rapid decrease is redundant. By defining as is natural the Fourier series of a periodic distribution \(w\) by the sequence (which can be shown to be of polynomial growth)
\[
\hat{w}(k):=(e^{-2\pi ikx},w)\quad k\in\Z^d
\tag{14}\]
and its \(\alpha\)-th distributional derivative by
\[(u,D^\alpha w):=(-1)^\abs{\alpha}(D^\alpha u,w)\]
we derive formulas analogous to the ones seen in the section on the Fourier transform for “periodic” distributions as well. Namely:
\[
w=\sum_{k\in\Z^d}\hat{w}(k)e^{2\pi i k x};\quad\widehat{D^\alpha w}(k)=(2\pi ik)^\alpha\hat{w}(k).
\tag{15}\]
To prove it all we have to do is apply Plancherel on \(u\) and move terms around via duality,
\[
\begin{aligned}
(u,w)&=(\sum_{k\in \Z^d}\hat{u}(k)e^{2\pi ik\cdot x},w )=\sum_{k\in \Z^d}  \hat{u}(k)\hat{w}(k)\\&
=\sum_{k\in \Z^d}  \left(\int_{\T^d} u(x)e^{-2\pi i\omega\cdot  x}dx\right)\hat{w}(k)=\int_{\T^d} u(x)\left(\sum_{k\in \Z^d} \hat{w}(k) e^{-2\pi i\omega\cdot  x}\right)dx.
\end{aligned}
\]
This proves the first part and the second can be proved directly by considering the relevant definitions.
To sum up what we have seen, due to the natural inclusion of integrable functions in the space of tempered distributions and the analogous inclusion in the periodic case, the notion of Fourier transform and differentiation extends to the larger space of tempered distributions. This allows us to manipulate rough functions (periodic or non-periodic) as if they had Fourier transforms and were smooth. As we shall see, this will prove of great use when obtaining “distributional solutions” to some PDEs. We now give the general method by which this achieved

Definition 6 Consider a mapping
\[P:A\subset\Ss(\R^d\to\C^m)\to \Ss(\R^d\to\C^m)\]
that extends to
\[P:S\subset \Ss'(\R^d\to\C^n)\to \Ss'(\R^d\to\C^n)\]
where \(S\) is some subset of \({\Ss'}(\R^d\to\C^m)\) containing \(\Ss^\infty(\R^d\to\C^m)\). Then, given \(f \in \Ss'(\R^d\to \C^n)\) we say that a distributional solution to \(Pw=f\) is any tempered distribution \(w\in S\) verifying \(Pw=f\).

In the above definition, \(P\) typically defines a linear or non-linear differential equation. Note that the above definition may be extended without any difficulty to the case of (periodic) distributional solutions in the case where \(P: C^\infty(\T^d\to\C^m)\to C^\infty(\T^d\to\R^n)\).

Example 1 Set \(P=\Delta\), as we have seen previously \(\Delta\) extends to \(S:=\Ss'(\R^d\to\C^m)\) with
\[
\Ff(\Delta \omega)=-4\pi \abs{\xi }^2\Ff w .
\]
Solving \(Pw=f\) formally yields \(w=\Ff^{-1}\left(\frac{-\Ff f}{4\pi^2 \abs{\xi }^2 }\right)\). However, division by \(\abs{\xi}^2\) is ill-defined at the origin in \(\Ss'(\R^d)\). A unique solution only exists if we restrict our domain (e.g., requiring \(\Ff f(0) = 0\) in suitable homogeneous spaces) to bypass the singularity.

objectID: posts/PDEs/1. The Fourier transform, tempered distributions and Sobolev spaces/index.html#sobolev-spaces
href: posts/PDEs/1. The Fourier transform, tempered distributions and Sobolev spaces/index.html#sobolev-spaces
title: PDEs 1: The Fourier transform, tempered distributions and Sobolev spaces
text:
4 Sobolev spaces
Sobolev spaces form a particular case of tempered distributions that we interpret as being smooth and integrable up to sufficient orders.

Definition 7 Given \(k\in\N^+\) we define the Sobolev space \(H^k(\R^d\to\C^m)\) as:
\[
\begin{aligned}
H^k(\R^d\to\C^m):=
\lbrace f\in &L^2(\R^d\to\C^m)\\&: D^\alpha f\in L^2(\R^d\to\C^m)\hookrightarrow\Ss '(\R^d\to\C^m)\quad\forall\abs{\alpha}\leq k\rbrace.
\end{aligned}
\]
Where, we consider \(L^2(\R^d\to\C^m)\) as a subspace of \(\Ss '(\R^d\to\C^m)\) using (11).

We may interpret the Sobolev space \(H^k(\R^d\to\C^m)\) as the space of \(k\) times differentiable functions in \(L^2(\R^d\to\C^m)\) and we give it the norm:
\[
\begin{aligned}
\norm{f}_{H^k(\R^d\to\C^m)}:=\sum_{\abs{\alpha}\leq k}\norm{D^\alpha f}_{L^2(\R^d\to\C^m)}\qquad f\in H^k(\R^d\to\C^m).
\end{aligned}
\tag{16}\]
Note that it is not enough to require \(\abs{\alpha}=k\) as, for example, the tempered distribution \(1 \in \Ss'(\R^d\to\C)\) has a derivative equal to zero however is not itself in \(L^2(\R^d\to\C)\). Now, by Plancherel for \(\Ss\) Lemma 1, the Fourier transform is an automorphism of \(\Ss'(\R^d\to\C^m)\), by using Proposition 3 we deduce that
\[D^\alpha f\in L^2(\R^d\to\C^m)\iff \Ff (D^\alpha f)=\abs{(2\pi i\xi )^{\alpha}} \hat{f}(\xi)\in L^2(\R^d\to\C^m)\]
from which we deduce that
\[
f\in H^k(\R^d\to\C^m)\iff \sum_{\abs{\alpha}\leq k} \norm{(2\pi i\xi )^{\alpha}\hat{f}(\xi)}_{L^2(\R^d)} \sim_k \norm{\br{\xi}^k\hat{f}}_{L^2(\R^d)} < \infty
\tag{17}\]
In fact, since the Fourier transform is a unitary transformation on \(L^2(\R^d\to\C^m)\), the same reasoning gives
\[
\norm{f}_{H^k(\R^d\to\C^m)}\sim_k \norm{\br{\xi}^k\hat{f}(\xi)}_{L^2(\R^d\to\C^m)}
\tag{18}\]
From the two equations above we deduce that if we define for a given real number \(s\) (including negative numbers!) the \(s\)-th order Sobolev space as
\[
\begin{aligned}
H^s(\R^d\to\C^m):=\lbrace f\in L^2(\R^d\to\C^m): \br{\xi}^s\hat{f}(\xi)\in L^2(\R^d\to\C^m)\rbrace
\end{aligned}
\tag{19}\]
and give it the norm
\[\norm{f}_{H^k(\R^d\to\C^m)}:= \norm{\br{\xi}^s\hat{f}(\xi)}_{L^2(\R^d\to\C^m)}\]
then our new definition is equivalent to the previous one (16) when \(s\) is a positive integer. We have thus found how to generalize the concept of Sobolev space to all real orders and obtained a useful way of characterizing them and giving a neat expression for their norm. Nonetheless, it will always be useful to retain the first definition based on derivatives, as it carries with it the motivation behind the definition of Sobolev spaces. As was the case with tempered distributions we can extend the concept of Sobolev space to periodic domains by defining given an integer \(k\) the Sobolev space \(H^k(\T^d\to\C^m)\) as the space of square-integrable \(\Z^d\) periodic functions with distributional derivatives themselves square integrable. Explicitly we define:
\[
\begin{aligned}
H^k(\T^d\to\C^m):=
\lbrace f\in &L^2(\T^d\to\C^m)\\&: D^\alpha f\in L^2(\T^d\to\C^m)\hookrightarrow\Ss '(\T^d\to\C^m)\quad\forall\abs{\alpha}\leq k\rbrace
\end{aligned}
\tag{20}\]
Using the same method as before, by the correspondence between regularity and decay for the Fourier transform of periodic functions Proposition 4 and Proposition 5, we deduce that
\[D^\alpha f\in H^k(\T^d\to\C^m)\iff\widehat{D^\alpha f}(k)=\abs{k^\alpha}\hat{f}(k)\in l^2(\Z^d\to\C^m)\]
which leads us as in the previous case to define for \(s\in\R\) the more general Sobolev space
\[H^s(\T^d\to\C^m):=\lbrace f\in L^2(\R^d\to\C^m): \br{k}^{s}\hat{f}(k)\in l^2(\Z^d\to\C^m)\rbrace\]
and to give it the norm
\[
\norm{f}_{H^s(\T^d\to\C^m)}:=\left(\sum_{k\in\Z^d}\br{k}^{2s}\abs{\hat{f}(k)}^2\right)^{\frac{1}{2}}
\tag{21}\]
where of course the definitions in (20) and (21) coincide for \(s\in\N\). Note that, by the previous discussion, we have that both in the euclidean and periodic case
\[
\begin{aligned}
f\in H^s(\R^d\to\C^m)  & \iff  D^\alpha f\in H^{s-\abs{\alpha}}(\R^d\to\C^m)\qquad\forall\abs{\alpha}\leq s\\
f\in H^s(\T^d\to\C^m) & \iff  D^\alpha f\in H^{s-\abs{\alpha}}(\T^d\to\C^m)\qquad\forall\abs{\alpha}\leq s
\end{aligned}
\tag{22}\]
One major advantage of working with the Sobolev spaces \(H^s\) is that, differently to the classical space of smooth functions \(C^s\), they form a Hilbert space with the inner product given by
\[
\begin{aligned}
\br{f,g}_{H^s(\R^d\to\C^m)}  & :=\int_{\R^d}\br{\xi }^{2s}\hat{f}(\xi )\overline{\hat{g}(\xi )} d\xi\\
\br{f,g}_{H^s(\T^d\to\C^m)} & :=\sum_{k\in \Z^d} \br{k }^{2s}\hat{f}(k)\overline{\widehat{g}(k )}.
\end{aligned}
\]
This gives one access to all the power of functional analysis and is invaluable in proofs. However, at the end of the day wishes to prove that solutions with smooth initial data are themselves smooth in a classical sense. This can be done by showing that the solution belongs to a Sobolev space of high enough order together with the following two results.

Lemma 2 (Sobolev Embedding into Continuous Functions) Given \(f\in H^{s}(\T^d\to\C^m)\) with \(s>d/2\). Then the Fourier series of \(f\) is absolutely convergent and \(f\in C(\T^d\to\C^m)\) with the bound
\[\norm{f}_{L^\infty(\T^d\to\C^m)}\lesssim_{d,s}\norm{f}_{H^s(\T^d\to\C^m)}\]


Proof. The proof is an application of the Cauchy-Schwartz inequality. We have that
\[
\begin{aligned}
\sum_{k\in\Z^d}\abs{\hat{f}(k)}&=\sum_{k\in\Z^d} \br{k}^{-s}\br{k}^{s}\abs{\hat{f}(k)}\leq\left(\sum_{k\in\Z^d} \frac{\br{k}^{-2s}}{2}\right)^{\frac{1}{2}}\left(\sum_{k\in\Z^d}\frac{\br{k}^{2s}}{2}\abs{\hat{f}(k)}^2\right)^{\frac{1}{2}}\\
&\lesssim_{d,s}\norm{f}_{H^s(\mathbb{T}^d\to\C^m)}<\infty.
\end{aligned}
\]
In consequence, the sum
\[
\sum_{k\in\Z^d}\hat{f}(k)e^{2\pi ik\cdot x}
\tag{23}\]
converges absolutely (and uniformly). Since by Plancherel’s Theorem, the above sum also converges in \(L^2(\T^d\to\C^m)\) to \(f\) we deduce that (23) converges almost everywhere to \(f\) (for example by taking a subsequence of the above sum that converges almost everywhere to \(f\)). Therefore
\[\norm{f}_{L^\infty(\T^d\to\C^m)}=\norm{\sum_{k\in\Z^d}\hat{f}(k)e^{2\pi ik\cdot x}}_{L^\infty(\T^d\to\C^m)}\]
which is
\[\leq\sum_{k\in\Z^d}\norm{\hat{f}(k)e^{2\pi ik\cdot x}}_{L^\infty(\T^d\to\C^m)}=\sum_{k\in\Z^d}\abs{\hat{f}(k)}\lesssim_{d,s}\norm{f}_{H^s(\T^d\to\C^m)}.\]
The continuity of \(f\) follows from the point-wise equality
\[
f(x)=\sum_{k\in\Z^d}\hat{f}(k)e^{2\pi ik\cdot x}=\int_{\Z^d} \hat{f}(k)e^{2\pi ik\cdot x} dk
\tag{24}\]
together with the monotone convergence theorem applied to \(\Z^d\) with the counting measure \(dk\).

As a corollary of this, we have the following two results

Proposition 6 (Sobolev embedding) Let \(f\in H^s(\T^d\to\C^m)\) where \(s>\frac{d}{2}+k\). Then \(f\in C^k(\T^d\to\C^m)\).


Proof. By (Lemma 2), we may apply the previous proposition to deduce that \(D^\alpha f\) is continuous for all \(\abs{\alpha}\leq k\). Therefore it suffices to show that for \(\abs{\alpha}\leq k\) the distributional derivatives \(D^\alpha f\) are also the classical derivatives of \(f\) which we denote by \(f_\alpha\). By the hypothesis placed on \(f\), we have that the series
\[\sum_{k\in\Z^d} (2\pi ik)^\alpha \hat{f}(k)e^{2\pi ik\cdot x} \]
is absolutely convergent (by Lemma 2), and hence, we may commute the derivatives of \(f\) with the sum in its Fourier series to deduce the point-wise equality
\[f_\alpha(x)=\sum_{k\in\Z^d} (2\pi ik)^\alpha \hat{f}(k)e^{2\pi ik\cdot x}.\]
Now, note that by using (15) for the Fourier coefficients of distributions we also have that the equality
\[D^\alpha f(x)=\sum_{k\in\Z^d} (2\pi ik)^\alpha \hat{f}(k)e^{2\pi ik\cdot x}\]
holds in \(L^2(\T^d\to\C^m)\). From these last two equalities, we deduce that \(f_\alpha=D^\alpha f\) almost everywhere which concludes our proof.

As before the previous results also have a euclidean analog whose proof is identical in replacing all of the above sums over \(\Z^d\) with integrals over \(\R^d\). Finally, we conclude this post with a neat little trick. Given smooth \(f\) and some differential operator \(\Ll =\sum_{\alpha} D^\alpha=p(D)\) we have that
\[
D^\alpha f =\Ff^{-1}(\Ff  \Ll f)=\Ff^{-1}(p_\Ll(2 \pi i\xi )\hat{f}(\xi ))
\]
The term \(p_\Ll\) is called a Fourier multiplier and there is no need to limit ourselves to polynomials. In fact, we may make the general definition that for a function of two variables \(p\)
\[
p(x,D)f:=\Ff^{-1}\left(p(x,\cdot  )\hat{f}(\cdot  )\right).
\]
This leads to the definition of pseudo-differential operators. A particular case is that of fractional operators. We now show an example.

Example 2 Given smooth \(f\) we have that
\[
-\Delta f=\Ff^{-1}(4 \pi^2 \abs{\xi}^2 \hat{f}(\xi )).
\]
As a result, we define the fractional Laplacian for all \(s\in \R\) as
\[
{(-\Delta)}^sf:=\Ff^{-1} \left(\left(4\pi^2 \abs{\xi }^2\right)^s\hat{f}(\xi )\right).
\]
By contrast, the Bessel potential operator is defined using the Japanese bracket: \((I - \frac{\Delta}{4\pi^2})^{s/2}f := \Ff^{-1}(\br{\xi}^s \hat{f}(\xi))\).

This post is already getting a bit long (if you’ve stuck in till the end I salute you), so we leave it off here with a neat little exercise (our first of this blog)

Exercise 1 How should we define \[T:=\left(1-\frac{\Delta}{4\pi ^2}  \right)^\frac{s}{2}\text{?} \] Once you do so show that for all \(s,k \in \R\), the operator \(T\) defines a linear bijective isometry
\[
T: H^k(\R^d\to\C^m)\to H^{k-s}(\R^d\to\C^m).
\]

In our future post we will discuss the well posedness of second order elliptic equations, and if we have time discuss the heat equation and how the Fourier transform affords provides us with an elegant solution.
